**Latvia**,[[b]](#cite_note-18) officially the **Republic of Latvia**,[[c]](#cite_note-19)[[17]](#cite_note-20) is a country in the [Baltic region](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region") of [northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe"). It is one of the three [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), along with [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") to the north and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") to the south. It borders [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") to the east and [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") to the southeast and shares a [maritime border](/wiki/Maritime_boundary "Maritime boundary") with [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") to the west. Latvia covers an area of 64,573 km2 (24,932 sq mi)[[18]](#cite_note-21), with a population of 1.83 million[[19]](#cite_note-22). The country has a [temperate seasonal climate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate").[[20]](#cite_note-23) Its capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Latvia "List of cities and towns in Latvia") is [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"). [Latvians](/wiki/Latvians "Latvians"), who are the [titular nation](/wiki/Titular_nation "Titular nation") and comprise 65.5% of the country's population, belong to the [ethnolinguistic group](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group") of the [Balts](/wiki/Balts "Balts") and speak [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"). [Russians](/wiki/Russians_in_Latvia "Russians in Latvia") are the most prominent minority in the country, at almost a quarter of the population; 37.7% of the population speak [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") as their native tongue.[[21]](#cite_note-eng.lsm.lv-24)

After centuries of [Teutonic](/wiki/State_of_the_Teutonic_Order "State of the Teutonic Order"), [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_Livonia "Swedish Livonia"), [Polish-Lithuanian](/wiki/Inflanty_Voivodeship "Inflanty Voivodeship"), and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") rule, the independent Republic of Latvia was established on 18 November 1918 after breaking away from the Russian Empire in the aftermath of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[3]](#cite_note-Germanis-4) The country became increasingly autocratic after the [coup in 1934](/wiki/1934_Latvian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1934 Latvian coup d'état") established the dictatorship of [Kārlis Ulmanis](/wiki/K%C4%81rlis_Ulmanis "Kārlis Ulmanis").[[22]](#cite_note-25) Latvia's *de facto* independence was interrupted at the outset of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), beginning with [forcible incorporation](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Latvia_in_1940 "Soviet occupation of Latvia in 1940") into the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), followed by the [invasion and occupation](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Latvia_during_World_War_II "German occupation of Latvia during World War II") by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in 1941 and the [re-occupation by the Soviets](/wiki/Soviet_re-occupation_of_Latvia_in_1944 "Soviet re-occupation of Latvia in 1944") in 1944, which formed the [Latvian SSR](/wiki/Latvian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Latvian Soviet Socialist Republic") for the next 45 years. As a result of extensive [immigration during the Soviet occupation](/wiki/Immigration_to_Latvia "Immigration to Latvia"), ethnic Russians became the most prominent minority in the country. The peaceful [Singing Revolution](/wiki/Singing_Revolution "Singing Revolution") started in 1987 among the Baltic Soviet republics and ended with the restoration of both *de facto* and [official independence](/wiki/State_continuity_of_the_Baltic_states "State continuity of the Baltic states") on 21 August 1991.[[d]](#cite_note-26) Latvia has since been a democratic [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic").

Latvia is a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with a [high-income](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy"), [advanced economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Latvia "Economy of Latvia") ranking 39th in the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"). It is a member of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [Council of the Baltic Sea States](/wiki/Council_of_the_Baltic_Sea_States "Council of the Baltic Sea States"), the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), the [Nordic-Baltic Eight](/wiki/Nordic-Baltic_Eight "Nordic-Baltic Eight"), the [Nordic Investment Bank](/wiki/Nordic_Investment_Bank "Nordic Investment Bank"), the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe"), and the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization").

## Etymology

The name *Latvija* is derived from the name of the ancient [Latgalians](/wiki/Latgalians "Latgalians"), one of four [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans") [Baltic tribes](/wiki/Balts "Balts") (along with [Curonians](/wiki/Curonians "Curonians"), [Selonians](/wiki/Selonians "Selonians") and [Semigallians](/wiki/Semigallians "Semigallians")), which formed the ethnic core of modern [Latvians](/wiki/Latvians "Latvians") together with the [Finnic](/wiki/Baltic_Finnic_peoples "Baltic Finnic peoples") [Livonians](/wiki/Livonians "Livonians").[[23]](#cite_note-27) [Henry of Latvia](/wiki/Henry_of_Latvia "Henry of Latvia") coined the [latinisations](/wiki/Latinisation_of_names "Latinisation of names") of the country's name, "Lettigallia" and "Lethia", both derived from the Latgalians. The terms inspired the variations on the country's name in [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") from "Letonia" and in several [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") from "Lettland".[[24]](#cite_note-28)

## History

Main article: [History of Latvia](/wiki/History_of_Latvia "History of Latvia")

Around 3000 BC, the Proto-Baltic ancestors of the Latvian people settled on the eastern coast of the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea").[[25]](#cite_note-29) The [Balts](/wiki/Balts "Balts") established trade routes to Rome and [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium"), trading local [amber](/wiki/Amber "Amber") for precious metals.[[26]](#cite_note-30) By 900 AD, four distinct Baltic tribes inhabited Latvia: [Curonians](/wiki/Curonians "Curonians"), [Latgalians](/wiki/Latgalians "Latgalians"), [Selonians](/wiki/Selonians "Selonians"), [Semigallians](/wiki/Semigallians "Semigallians") (in Latvian: *kurši*, *latgaļi*, *sēļi* and *zemgaļi*), as well as the Finnic tribe of [Livonians](/wiki/Livonian_people "Livonian people") (*lībieši*) speaking a Finnic language.[[27]](#cite_note-31)

In the 12th century in the territory of Latvia, there were lands with their rulers: [Vanema](/w/index.php?title=Vanema&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vanema (page does not exist)") [[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vanema "lv:Vanema")], [Ventava](/w/index.php?title=Ventava_(land)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ventava (land) (page does not exist)") [[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ventava "lv:Ventava")], [Bandava](/wiki/Bandava "Bandava"), [Piemare](/wiki/Piemare "Piemare"), [Duvzare](/w/index.php?title=Duvzare&action=edit&redlink=1 "Duvzare (page does not exist)") [[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Duvzare "lv:Duvzare")], [Selen](/w/index.php?title=Selen_(land)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Selen (land) (page does not exist)") [[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%C4%93lija_(zeme) "lv:Sēlija (zeme)")], [Koknese](/wiki/Principality_of_Koknese "Principality of Koknese"), [Jersika](/wiki/Jersika "Jersika"), [Tālava](/wiki/T%C4%81lava "Tālava") and [Atzele](/w/index.php?title=Atzele&action=edit&redlink=1 "Atzele (page does not exist)") [[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atzele "lv:Atzele")].[[28]](#cite_note-32)

### Medieval period

Although the local people had contact with the outside world for centuries, they became more fully integrated into the European socio-political system in the 12th century.[[29]](#cite_note-33) The first missionaries, sent by the pope, sailed up the [Daugava](/wiki/Daugava "Daugava") River in the late 12th century, seeking converts.[[30]](#cite_note-Lonely-34) The local people, however, did not convert to Christianity as readily as the church had hoped.[[30]](#cite_note-Lonely-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Burg_Turaida04.jpg/250px-Burg_Turaida04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burg_Turaida04.jpg)

[Turaida Castle](/wiki/Turaida_Castle "Turaida Castle") near [Sigulda](/wiki/Sigulda "Sigulda"), built in 1214 under [Albert of Riga](/wiki/Albert_of_Riga "Albert of Riga")

At the beginning of the 13th century, Germans ruled large parts of what is currently Latvia.[[30]](#cite_note-Lonely-34) The influx of German crusaders especially increased in the second half of the 13th century following the [decline and fall of the Crusader States](/wiki/Crusades#Crusades_and_the_Holy_Land,_1095–1291 "Crusades") in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East").[[31]](#cite_note-35) Together with southern Estonia, these conquered areas formed the [crusader state](/wiki/Crusader_states "Crusader states") that became known as [Terra Mariana](/wiki/Terra_Mariana "Terra Mariana") ([Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") for "Land of [Mary](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)")") or Livonia.[[32]](#cite_note-36) In 1282, [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), and later the cities of [Cēsis](/wiki/C%C4%93sis "Cēsis"), [Limbaži](/wiki/Limba%C5%BEi "Limbaži"), [Koknese](/wiki/Koknese "Koknese") and [Valmiera](/wiki/Valmiera "Valmiera"), became part of the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League").[[30]](#cite_note-Lonely-34) Riga became an important point of east–west trading[[30]](#cite_note-Lonely-34) and formed close cultural links with [western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe").[[33]](#cite_note-37) The first German settlers were knights from northern Germany and citizens of northern German towns who brought their [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") language to the region, which shaped many loanwords in the Latvian language.[[34]](#cite_note-38)

### Reformation period and Polish and Swedish rule

Main articles: [Swedish Livonia](/wiki/Swedish_Livonia "Swedish Livonia"), [Duchy of Courland and Semigallia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Courland_and_Semigallia "Duchy of Courland and Semigallia"), [Duchy of Livonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Livonia "Duchy of Livonia"), and [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/dd/Polish-Lithuanian_Commonwealth_at_its_maximum_extent.svg/250px-Polish-Lithuanian_Commonwealth_at_its_maximum_extent.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Polish-Lithuanian_Commonwealth_at_its_maximum_extent.svg)

The [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") at its largest extent; modern-day boundaries are also shown.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Swedish_Empire.svg/250px-Swedish_Empire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Swedish_Empire.svg)

The [Swedish Empire](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") (1560–1815)

After the [Livonian War](/wiki/Livonian_War "Livonian War") (1558–1583), Livonia (northern Latvia and southern Estonia) fell under the hegemony of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth").[[30]](#cite_note-Lonely-34) The lands were ceded to the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") and formed into the [Duchy of Livonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Livonia "Duchy of Livonia") (*Ducatus Livoniae Ultradunensis*). [Gotthard Kettler](/wiki/Gotthard_Kettler "Gotthard Kettler"), the last master of the [Order of Livonia](/wiki/Livonian_Order "Livonian Order"), formed the [Duchy of Courland and Semigallia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Courland_and_Semigallia "Duchy of Courland and Semigallia").[[35]](#cite_note-39) Though the duchy was a vassal state to the Lithuanian Grand Duchy and later of Poland–Lithuania, it retained a considerable degree of autonomy and experienced a golden age in the 16th century. [Latgalia](/wiki/Latgalia "Latgalia"), the easternmost region of Latvia, became a part of the [Inflanty Voivodeship](/wiki/Inflanty_Voivodeship "Inflanty Voivodeship") of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.[[36]](#cite_note-40)

In the 17th and early 18th centuries, the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire"), and [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") struggled for supremacy in the eastern Baltic. After the [Polish–Swedish War](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Swedish_War_(1600%E2%80%931611) "Polish–Swedish War (1600–1611)"), northern Livonia (including Vidzeme) came under Swedish rule. Riga became the capital of [Swedish Livonia](/wiki/Swedish_Livonia "Swedish Livonia") and the largest city in the entire Swedish Empire.[[37]](#cite_note-41) Fighting continued sporadically between Sweden and Poland until the [Truce of Altmark](/wiki/Truce_of_Altmark "Truce of Altmark") in 1629.[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43) In Latvia, the Swedish period is generally remembered as positive; serfdom was eased, a network of schools was established for the peasantry, and the power of the regional [barons](/wiki/Baltic_Germans "Baltic Germans") was diminished.[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45)

Several important cultural changes occurred during this time. Under Swedish and largely German rule, western Latvia adopted [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") as its main religion.[[42]](#cite_note-46) The ancient tribes of the Couronians, Semigallians, Selonians, Livs, and northern Latgallians assimilated to form the Latvian people, speaking one Latvian language.[[43]](#cite_note-47)[[44]](#cite_note-48) An actual Latvian state had not been established, so the borders and definitions of who fell within that group are largely subjective. Meanwhile, largely isolated from the rest of Latvia, southern Latgallians adopted [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") under Polish/[Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") influence. The native dialect remained distinct, although it acquired many Polish and Russian loanwords.[[45]](#cite_note-49)

### Livonia and Courland in the Russian Empire

During the [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War") (1700–1721), up to 40 percent of Latvians died from famine and plague.[[46]](#cite_note-50) Half the residents of Riga were killed by [plague in 1710–1711](/wiki/Great_Northern_War_plague_outbreak "Great Northern War plague outbreak").[[47]](#cite_note-51) The [capitulation of Estonia and Livonia](/wiki/Capitulation_of_Estonia_and_Livonia "Capitulation of Estonia and Livonia") in 1710 and the [Treaty of Nystad](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nystad "Treaty of Nystad"), ending the Great Northern War in 1721, gave Vidzeme to Russia (it became part of the [Riga Governorate](/wiki/Governorate_of_Livonia "Governorate of Livonia")). The Latgale region remained part of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth as Inflanty Voivodeship until 1772, when it was incorporated into Russia. The [Duchy of Courland and Semigallia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Courland_and_Semigallia "Duchy of Courland and Semigallia"), a vassal state of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, was annexed by Russia in 1795 in the [Third Partition of Poland](/wiki/Third_Partition_of_Poland "Third Partition of Poland"), bringing all of what is now Latvia into the Russian Empire. All three Baltic provinces preserved local laws, German as the local [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and their own parliament, the [Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag").

The emancipation of the serfs took place in Courland in 1817 and in Vidzeme in 1819.[[48]](#cite_note-52) In practice, however, the emancipation was actually advantageous to the landowners and nobility, as it dispossessed peasants of their land without compensation, forcing them to return to work at the estates "of their own free will".[[49]](#cite_note-53) During these two centuries Latvia experienced economic and construction boom – ports were expanded (Riga became the largest port in the Russian Empire), railways built; new factories, banks, and a university were established; many residential, public (theatres and museums), and school buildings were erected; new parks formed; and so on. Riga's boulevards and some streets outside the Old Town date from this period. [Numeracy](/wiki/Numeracy "Numeracy") was also higher in the Livonian and Courlandian parts of the Russian Empire, which may have been influenced by the Protestant religion of the inhabitants.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Latvians_national_rally_in_Dundaga_in_1905.jpg/250px-Latvians_national_rally_in_Dundaga_in_1905.jpg)](/wiki/File:Latvians_national_rally_in_Dundaga_in_1905.jpg)

Latvians national rally in [Dundaga](/wiki/Dundaga "Dundaga") in 1905

During the 19th century, the social structure changed dramatically.[[51]](#cite_note-LNA-55) A class of independent farmers established itself after reforms allowed the peasants to repurchase their land, but many landless peasants remained. Many Latvians left for the cities and sought education and industrial jobs.[[51]](#cite_note-LNA-55) There also developed a growing urban [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") and an increasingly influential Latvian [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie").[[51]](#cite_note-LNA-55) The [Young Latvian](/wiki/Young_Latvians "Young Latvians") ([Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *Jaunlatvieši*) movement laid the groundwork for nationalism from the middle of the century, many of its leaders looking to the [Slavophiles](/wiki/Slavophilia "Slavophilia") for support against the prevailing German-dominated social order.[[52]](#cite_note-YoungLatvians-56)[[53]](#cite_note-57) The rise in use of the Latvian language in literature and society became known as the [First National Awakening](/wiki/Latvian_National_Awakening "Latvian National Awakening").[[52]](#cite_note-YoungLatvians-56) [Russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification") began in Latgale after the Polish led the [January Uprising](/wiki/January_Uprising "January Uprising") in 1863: this spread to the rest of what is now Latvia by the 1880s. The Young Latvians were largely eclipsed by the [New Current](/wiki/New_Current "New Current"), a broad leftist social and political movement, in the 1890s.[[54]](#cite_note-58) Popular discontent exploded in the [1905 Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_of_1905 "Russian Revolution of 1905"), which took a nationalist character in the Baltic provinces.[[55]](#cite_note-59)[[56]](#cite_note-60)[[57]](#cite_note-61)

### Declaration of independence and interwar period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Janis_Cakste.jpg/250px-Janis_Cakste.jpg)](/wiki/File:Janis_Cakste.jpg)

[Jānis Čakste](/wiki/J%C4%81nis_%C4%8Cakste "Jānis Čakste") (1859–1927), the first [president of Latvia](/wiki/President_of_Latvia "President of Latvia")

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") devastated the territory[[58]](#cite_note-62) and other western parts of the Russian Empire. Demands for [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") were initially confined to [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), until a power vacuum was created by the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") in 1917, followed by the [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk") between Russia and Germany in March 1918, then the Allied [armistice with Germany on 11 November 1918](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918"). The [People's Council of Latvia](/wiki/People%27s_Council_of_Latvia "People's Council of Latvia") proclaimed the independence, and [Kārlis Ulmanis](/wiki/K%C4%81rlis_Ulmanis "Kārlis Ulmanis") was entrusted to set up a government and he took the position of prime minister.[[59]](#cite_note-63) The general representative of Germany [August Winnig](/wiki/August_Winnig "August Winnig") formally handed over political power to the Latvian provisional government on 26 November, and he became its first prime minister.[[60]](#cite_note-64)

The [war of independence](/wiki/Latvian_War_of_Independence "Latvian War of Independence") that followed was part of a general chaotic period of civil and new border wars in eastern Europe. By the spring of 1919, there were actually three governments: the provisional government headed by Ulmanis, supported by the [Tautas padome](/wiki/Tautas_padome "Tautas padome") and the [Inter-Allied Commission of Control](/wiki/Military_Inter-Allied_Commission_of_Control "Military Inter-Allied Commission of Control"); the [Latvian Soviet government](/wiki/Latvian_Socialist_Soviet_Republic "Latvian Socialist Soviet Republic") led by [Pēteris Stučka](/wiki/P%C4%93teris_Stu%C4%8Dka "Pēteris Stučka"), supported by the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army"); and the provisional government headed by [Andrievs Niedra](/wiki/Andrievs_Niedra "Andrievs Niedra"), supported by [Baltic-German forces](/wiki/Freikorps_in_the_Baltic "Freikorps in the Baltic") composed of the *[Baltische Landeswehr](/wiki/Baltische_Landeswehr "Baltische Landeswehr")* ("Baltic Defence Force") and the [Freikorps](/wiki/Freikorps "Freikorps") formation *[Eiserne Division](/wiki/Eiserne_Division "Eiserne Division")* ("Iron Division"). Estonian and Latvian forces defeated the Germans at the [Battle of Wenden](/wiki/Battle_of_C%C4%93sis_(1919) "Battle of Cēsis (1919)") in June 1919,[[61]](#cite_note-65) and a massive attack by a predominantly German force—the [West Russian Volunteer Army](/wiki/West_Russian_Volunteer_Army "West Russian Volunteer Army")—under [Pavel Bermondt-Avalov](/wiki/Pavel_Bermondt-Avalov "Pavel Bermondt-Avalov") was repelled in November. Eastern Latvia was cleared of Red Army forces by Latvian and Polish troops in early 1920 (from the Polish perspective the [Battle of Daugavpils](/wiki/Battle_of_Daugavpils "Battle of Daugavpils") was a part of the [Polish–Soviet War](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Polish–Soviet War")).

A freely elected [constituent assembly](/wiki/Constituent_assembly "Constituent assembly") convened on 1 May 1920 and adopted a liberal constitution, the *[Satversme](/wiki/Constitution_of_Latvia "Constitution of Latvia")*, in February 1922.[[62]](#cite_note-66) On 15 May 1934, Ulmanis staged [a bloodless coup](/wiki/1934_Latvian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1934 Latvian coup d'état"), establishing a nationalist dictatorship that lasted until 1940.[[63]](#cite_note-Country_profile-67) After 1934, Ulmanis established [government corporations](/wiki/Government_corporation "Government corporation") to buy up private firms with the aim of "Latvianising" the economy.[[64]](#cite_note-68) The constitution was partly suspended by Ulmanis after his coup in 1934 but reaffirmed in 1990. Since then, it has been amended and is still in effect in Latvia today. With most of Latvia's industrial base evacuated to the interior of Russia in 1915, radical land reform was the central political question for the young state. In 1897, 61.2% of the rural population had been landless; by 1936, that percentage had been reduced to 18%.[[65]](#cite_note-69)

### Occupations, 1940–1990

Main articles: [Soviet occupation of Latvia in 1940](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Latvia_in_1940 "Soviet occupation of Latvia in 1940"), [German occupation of Latvia during World War II](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Latvia_during_World_War_II "German occupation of Latvia during World War II"), [Occupation of Latvia by Soviet Union 1944–1945](/wiki/Occupation_of_Latvia_by_Soviet_Union_1944%E2%80%931945 "Occupation of Latvia by Soviet Union 1944–1945"), and [Latvian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Latvian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Latvian Soviet Socialist Republic")

See also: [The Holocaust in Latvia](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Latvia "The Holocaust in Latvia"), [Latvian partisans](/wiki/Latvian_partisans "Latvian partisans"), and [Latvian anti-Nazi resistance movement 1941–45](/wiki/Latvian_anti-Nazi_resistance_movement_1941%E2%80%9345 "Latvian anti-Nazi resistance movement 1941–45")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Riga_1940_Soviet_Army.jpg/250px-Riga_1940_Soviet_Army.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riga_1940_Soviet_Army.jpg)

[Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") troops enter [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") (1940).

Early in the morning of 24 August 1939, the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") signed a 10-year non-aggression pact, called the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact").[[66]](#cite_note-70) The pact contained a secret protocol, revealed only after Germany's defeat in 1945, according to which the states of Northern and Eastern Europe were divided into German and Soviet "[spheres of influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence")".[[67]](#cite_note-mrtext-71) In the north, Latvia, Finland and Estonia were assigned to the Soviet sphere.[[67]](#cite_note-mrtext-71)

After the conclusion of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, most of the [Baltic Germans](/wiki/Baltic_Germans "Baltic Germans") left Latvia by agreement between Ulmanis's government and Nazi Germany under the [Heim ins Reich](/wiki/Heim_ins_Reich "Heim ins Reich") programme.[[68]](#cite_note-Latvia_in_WWII-72) Most of those who remained left for Germany in summer 1940, when a second resettlement scheme was agreed.[[69]](#cite_note-73) The racially approved being resettled mainly in Poland, being given land and businesses in exchange for the money they had received from the sale of their previous assets.[[70]](#cite_note-PBBG-74): 46

On 5 October 1939, Latvia was forced to accept a "mutual assistance" pact with the Soviet Union, granting the Soviets the right to station between 25,000 and 30,000 troops on Latvian territory.[[71]](#cite_note-75) State administrators were murdered and replaced by Soviet cadres.[[72]](#cite_note-wettig20-76) Elections were held with single pro-Soviet candidates listed for many positions. The resulting people's assembly immediately requested admission into the USSR, which the Soviet Union granted.[[72]](#cite_note-wettig20-76) Latvia, then a puppet government, was headed by [Augusts Kirhenšteins](/wiki/Augusts_Kirhen%C5%A1teins "Augusts Kirhenšteins").[[73]](#cite_note-77) The Soviet Union incorporated Latvia on 5 August 1940, as the *[Latvian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Latvian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Latvian Soviet Socialist Republic").*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-L19397%2C_Lettland%2C_Riga%2C_Begr%C3%BC%C3%9Fung_der_deutschen_Soldaten.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-L19397%2C_Lettland%2C_Riga%2C_Begr%C3%BC%C3%9Fung_der_deutschen_Soldaten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-L19397,_Lettland,_Riga,_Begr%C3%BC%C3%9Fung_der_deutschen_Soldaten.jpg)

German soldiers enter Riga, July 1941.

The Soviets dealt harshly with their [perceived opponents](/wiki/Enemy_of_the_people#Soviet_Union "Enemy of the people") – prior to [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"), in less than a year, at least 34,250 Latvians were [deported](/wiki/June_deportation "June deportation") or killed.[[74]](#cite_note-78) Most were deported to Siberia where deaths were estimated at 40 percent.[[70]](#cite_note-PBBG-74): 48

On 22 June 1941, German troops attacked Soviet forces in Operation Barbarossa.[[75]](#cite_note-79) There were some spontaneous uprisings by Latvians against the Red Army which helped the Germans. By 29 June [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") was reached and with Soviet troops killed, captured or retreating, Latvia was left under the control of German forces by early July.[[76]](#cite_note-80)[[70]](#cite_note-PBBG-74): 78–96

Under German occupation, Latvia was administered as part of *[Reichskommissariat Ostland](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ostland "Reichskommissariat Ostland")*.[[77]](#cite_note-81) Latvian paramilitary and [Auxiliary Police](/wiki/Arajs_Kommando "Arajs Kommando") units established by the occupation authority participated in [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") and other atrocities.[[63]](#cite_note-Country_profile-67) 30,000 Jews were shot in Latvia in the autumn of 1941.[[70]](#cite_note-PBBG-74): 127 Another 30,000 Jews from the Riga ghetto were killed in the Rumbula Forest in November and December 1941, to reduce overpopulation in the ghetto and make room for more Jews being brought in from Germany and the West.[[70]](#cite_note-PBBG-74): 128 There was a pause in fighting, apart from partisan activity, until after the [siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Leningrad "Battle of Leningrad") ended in January 1944, and the Soviet troops advanced, entering Latvia in July and eventually capturing Riga on 13 October 1944.[[70]](#cite_note-PBBG-74): 271

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Red_Army_soldiers_in_Riga._October_1944.jpg/250px-Red_Army_soldiers_in_Riga._October_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_Army_soldiers_in_Riga._October_1944.jpg)

[Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") soldiers in front of the [Freedom Monument](/wiki/Freedom_Monument "Freedom Monument") in Riga in 1944

The Soviets reoccupied the country in 1944–1945, and further [deportations](/wiki/Operation_Priboi "Operation Priboi") followed as the country was [collectivised](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union")
and [Sovietised](/wiki/Sovietisation "Sovietisation").[[63]](#cite_note-Country_profile-67)

In the post-war period, Latvia was made to adopt Soviet farming methods. Rural areas were forced into [collectivization](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union").[[78]](#cite_note-82) An extensive program to impose [bilingualism](/wiki/Bilingualism "Bilingualism") was initiated in Latvia, limiting the use of Latvian language in official uses in favor of using Russian as the main language. All of the minority schools (Jewish, Polish, Belarusian, Estonian, Lithuanian) were closed down leaving only two media of instructions in the schools: Latvian and Russian.[[79]](#cite_note-83) An influx of new colonists, including laborers, administrators, military personnel and their dependents from Russia and other Soviet republics started. By 1959 about 400,000 Russian settlers arrived and the ethnic Latvian population had fallen to 62%.[[80]](#cite_note-84)

Since Latvia had maintained a well-developed infrastructure and educated specialists, Moscow decided to base some of the Soviet Union's most advanced manufacturing in Latvia. New industry was created in Latvia, including a major [machinery factory RAF](/wiki/Riga_Autobus_Factory "Riga Autobus Factory") in [Jelgava](/wiki/Jelgava "Jelgava"), electrotechnical factories in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), chemical factories in [Daugavpils](/wiki/Daugavpils "Daugavpils"), [Valmiera](/wiki/Valmiera "Valmiera") and [Olaine](/wiki/Olaine "Olaine")—and some food and oil processing plants.[[81]](#cite_note-85) Latvia manufactured trains, ships, minibuses, mopeds, telephones, radios and hi-fi systems, electrical and diesel engines, textiles, furniture, clothing, bags and luggage, shoes, musical instruments, home appliances, watches, tools and equipment, aviation and agricultural equipment and long list of other goods. Latvia had its own film industry and musical records factory (LPs). However, there were not enough people to operate the newly built factories.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] To maintain and expand industrial production, skilled workers were migrating from all over the Soviet Union, decreasing the proportion of ethnic Latvians in the republic.[[82]](#cite_note-86) The population of Latvia reached its peak in 1990 at just under 2.7 million people.

In the second half of the 1980s, Soviet leader [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") started to introduce political and economic reforms in the Soviet Union that were called [glasnost](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost") and [perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika"). In the summer of 1987, the first large demonstrations were held in Riga at the [Freedom Monument](/wiki/Freedom_Monument "Freedom Monument")—a symbol of independence. In the summer of 1988, a national movement, coalescing in the [Popular Front of Latvia](/wiki/Popular_Front_of_Latvia "Popular Front of Latvia"), was opposed by the [Interfront](/wiki/International_Front_of_the_Working_People_of_Latvia "International Front of the Working People of Latvia"). The Latvian SSR, along with the other [Baltic Republics](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") was allowed greater autonomy, and in 1988, the old pre-war [Flag of Latvia](/wiki/Flag_of_Latvia "Flag of Latvia") flew again, replacing the Soviet Latvian flag as the official flag in 1990.[[83]](#cite_note-87)[[84]](#cite_note-88) In 1989, the [Supreme Soviet of the USSR](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_USSR "Supreme Soviet of the USSR") adopted a resolution on the *[Occupation of the Baltic states](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states")*, in which it declared the occupation "not in accordance with law", and not the "will of the Soviet people". Pro-independence Popular Front of Latvia candidates gained a two-thirds majority in the [Supreme Council](/wiki/Supreme_Council_of_the_Republic_of_Latvia "Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia") in the [March 1990 democratic elections](/wiki/1990_Latvian_Supreme_Soviet_election "1990 Latvian Supreme Soviet election").

### 1990–present

Further information: [Singing Revolution](/wiki/Singing_Revolution "Singing Revolution"), [Baltic Way](/wiki/Baltic_Way "Baltic Way"), and [On the Restoration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia](/wiki/On_the_Restoration_of_Independence_of_the_Republic_of_Latvia "On the Restoration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Riga_barricade_1991.jpg/250px-Riga_barricade_1991.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riga_barricade_1991.jpg)

Barricade in Riga to prevent the [Soviet Army](/wiki/Soviet_Army "Soviet Army") from reaching the [Latvian Parliament](/wiki/Saeima "Saeima") in July 1991

On 4 May 1990, the Supreme Council adopted the [Declaration on the Restoration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Restoration_of_Independence_of_the_Republic_of_Latvia "Declaration on the Restoration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia"), and the Latvian SSR was renamed Republic of Latvia.[[85]](#cite_note-Imagining-89)

However, the central power in Moscow continued to regard Latvia as a Soviet republic in 1990 and 1991. In January 1991, Soviet political and military forces unsuccessfully tried to overthrow the Republic of Latvia authorities by occupying the central publishing house in Riga and establishing a Committee of National Salvation to usurp governmental functions. During the transitional period, Moscow maintained many central Soviet state authorities in Latvia.[[85]](#cite_note-Imagining-89)

[The Popular Front of Latvia](/wiki/Popular_Front_of_Latvia "Popular Front of Latvia") advocated that all permanent residents be eligible for Latvian citizenship, however, universal citizenship for all permanent residents was not adopted. Instead, citizenship was granted to persons who had been citizens of Latvia on the day of loss of independence in 1940 as well as their descendants. As a consequence, the majority of ethnic non-Latvians did not receive Latvian citizenship since neither they nor their parents had ever been citizens of Latvia, becoming [non-citizens](/wiki/Non-citizens_(Latvia) "Non-citizens (Latvia)") or citizens of other former Soviet republics. By 2011, more than half of non-citizens had taken [naturalization](/wiki/Naturalization "Naturalization") exams and received Latvian citizenship, but in 2015 there were still 290,660 non-citizens in Latvia, which represented 14.1% of the population. They have [no citizenship of any country](/wiki/Statelessness "Statelessness"), and cannot participate in the parliamentary elections.[[86]](#cite_note-90) Children born to non-nationals or stateless persons after the re-establishment of independence on 21 August 1991 are automatically entitled to citizenship.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

The Republic of Latvia declared the end of the transitional period and restored full independence on 21 August 1991, in the aftermath of the failed [Soviet coup attempt](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt").[[4]](#cite_note-FINemb-5) Latvia resumed diplomatic relations with Western states, including Sweden.[[88]](#cite_note-92) The [Saeima](/wiki/Saeima "Saeima"), Latvia's parliament, was again elected in 1993. Russia ended its military presence by completing its troop withdrawal in 1994 and shutting down the [Skrunda-1](/wiki/Skrunda-1 "Skrunda-1") radar station in 1998.

The major goals of Latvia in the 1990s, to join [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), were achieved in 2004. The [NATO Summit 2006](/wiki/NATO_Summit_2006 "NATO Summit 2006") was held in Riga.[[89]](#cite_note-93) [Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga](/wiki/Vaira_V%C4%AB%C4%B7e-Freiberga "Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga") was [President of Latvia](/wiki/President_of_Latvia "President of Latvia") from 1999 until 2007. She was the first female head of state in the former Soviet bloc state and was active in Latvia joining both NATO and the European Union in 2004.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Latvia signed the [Schengen agreement](/wiki/Schengen_Agreement "Schengen Agreement") on 16 April 2003 and started its implementation on 21 December 2007.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Saeimas_18.novembra_svin%C4%ABg%C4%81_s%C4%93de_%2845023401235%29.jpg/250px-Saeimas_18.novembra_svin%C4%ABg%C4%81_s%C4%93de_%2845023401235%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saeimas_18.novembra_svin%C4%ABg%C4%81_s%C4%93de_(45023401235).jpg)

The ceremonial meeting of the [Saeima](/wiki/Saeima "Saeima") in 2018 in honor of the 100th anniversary of the proclamation of the Republic of Latvia at the [Latvian National Theatre](/wiki/Latvian_National_Theatre "Latvian National Theatre"), where the country was [founded on 18 November 1918](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Latvia) "Independence Day (Latvia)")

Approximately 72% of Latvian citizens are Latvian, while 20% are Russian.[[92]](#cite_note-96) The government denationalized private property confiscated by the Soviets, returning it or compensating the owners for it, and [privatized](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization") most state-owned industries, reintroducing the [prewar currency](/wiki/Latvian_lats "Latvian lats"). Latvia is one of the fastest growing economies in the European Union,[[93]](#cite_note-97) despite its difficult re-orientation towards Western Europe and economic liberalization. In November 2013, [the roof collapsed](/wiki/Zolit%C5%ABde_shopping_centre_roof_collapse "Zolitūde shopping centre roof collapse") at a shopping center in Riga, causing Latvia's worst post-independence disaster with the deaths of 54 rush hour shoppers and rescue personnel.[[94]](#cite_note-98)

In late 2018 the National Archives of Latvia released a full alphabetical index of some 10,000 people recruited as agents or informants by the Soviet KGB. The publication took place after two decades of public debate, as well as the passage of a special law. As a result, the names, code names, birthplaces, and other data on active and former KGB agents (as of 1991, when Latvia regained independence) were revealed.[[95]](#cite_note-99)

In May 2023, the parliament elected [Edgars Rinkēvičs](/wiki/Edgars_Rink%C4%93vi%C4%8Ds "Edgars Rinkēvičs") as new [President of Latvia](/wiki/President_of_Latvia "President of Latvia"), making him the European Union's first openly gay head of state.[[96]](#cite_note-100) After years of debates, Latvia ratified the EU *Convention on Preventing and Combating Violence Against Women and Domestic Violence*, otherwise known as the [Istanbul Convention](/wiki/Istanbul_Convention "Istanbul Convention") in November 2023.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Latvia](/wiki/Geography_of_Latvia "Geography of Latvia")

See also: [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"), [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), and [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/BalticSea_March2000_NASA-S2000084115409.png/250px-BalticSea_March2000_NASA-S2000084115409.png)](/wiki/File:BalticSea_March2000_NASA-S2000084115409.png)

Latvia lies in Northern Europe, on the eastern shores of the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea").

Latvia lies in Northern Europe, on the eastern shores of the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and northwestern part of the [East European Craton](/wiki/East_European_craton "East European craton") (EEC), between latitudes [55°](/wiki/55th_parallel_north "55th parallel north") and [58° N](/wiki/58th_parallel_north "58th parallel north") (a small area is north of 58°), and longitudes [21°](/wiki/21st_meridian_east "21st meridian east") and [29° E](/wiki/29th_meridian_east "29th meridian east") (a small area is west of 21°). Latvia has a total area of 64,559 km2 (24,926 sq mi) of which 62,157 km2 (23,999 sq mi) land, 18,159 km2 (7,011 sq mi) agricultural land,[[98]](#cite_note-Agriculture_–_Key_Indicators-102) 34,964 km2 (13,500 sq mi) forest land[[99]](#cite_note-Forestry_–_Key_Indicators-103) and 2,402 km2 (927 sq mi) inland water.[[100]](#cite_note-Geographical_Data_–_Key_Indicators-104)

The total length of Latvia's boundary is 1,866 km (1,159 mi). The total length of its land boundary is 1,368 km (850 mi), of which 343 km (213 mi) is shared with [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") to the north, 276 km (171 mi) with the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russian_Federation "Russian Federation") to the east, 161 km (100 mi) with [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") to the southeast and 588 km (365 mi) with [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") to the south. The total length of its [maritime boundary](/wiki/Maritime_boundary "Maritime boundary") is 498 km (309 mi), which is shared with Estonia, Sweden and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"). Extension from north to south is 210 km (130 mi) and from west to east 450 km (280 mi).[[100]](#cite_note-Geographical_Data_–_Key_Indicators-104)

Most of Latvia's territory is less than 100 m (330 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"). Its largest lake, [Lubāns](/wiki/Lub%C4%81ns "Lubāns"), has an area of 80.7 km2 (31.2 sq mi), its deepest lake, [Drīdzis](/wiki/Dr%C4%ABdzis "Drīdzis"), is 65.1 m (214 ft) deep. The longest river on Latvian territory is the [Gauja](/wiki/Gauja "Gauja"), at 452 km (281 mi) in length. The longest river flowing through Latvian territory is the [Daugava](/wiki/Daugava_River "Daugava River"), which has a total length of 1,005 km (624 mi), of which 352 km (219 mi) is on Latvian territory. Latvia's highest point is [Gaiziņkalns](/wiki/Gaizi%C5%86kalns "Gaiziņkalns"), 311.6 m (1,022 ft). The length of Latvia's [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") coastline is 494 km (307 mi). An inlet of the Baltic Sea, the shallow [Gulf of Riga](/wiki/Gulf_of_Riga "Gulf of Riga") is situated in the northwest of the country.[[101]](#cite_note-Latvia_in_brief-105)

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Koppen_World_Map_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png/250px-Koppen_World_Map_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen_World_Map_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png)

Humid continental climate warm summer subtype

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Koppen_classification_worldmap_CfbCfc.png/250px-Koppen_classification_worldmap_CfbCfc.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen_classification_worldmap_CfbCfc.png)

Oceanic climate

Latvia has a [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate") climate that has been described in various sources as either [humid continental](/wiki/Humid_continental "Humid continental") ([Köppen *Dfb*](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate#Dfb/Dwb/Dsb:_Warm_summer_subtype "Humid continental climate")) or [oceanic/maritime](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (Köppen *Cfb*).[[102]](#cite_note-liepu-106)[[103]](#cite_note-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108)

Coastal regions, especially the western coast of the [Courland Peninsula](/wiki/Courland_Peninsula "Courland Peninsula"), possess a more maritime climate with cooler summers and milder winters, while eastern parts exhibit a more continental climate with warmer summers and harsher winters.[[102]](#cite_note-liepu-106) Nevertheless, the temperature variations are little as the territory of Latvia is relatively small.[[105]](#cite_note-LatviaClimate-109) Moreover, Latvia's terrain is particularly flat (no more than 350 meters high), thus the Latvian climate is not differentiated by altitude.[[105]](#cite_note-LatviaClimate-109)

Latvia has four pronounced seasons of near-equal length. Winter starts in mid-December and lasts until mid-March. Winters have average temperatures of −6 °C (21 °F) and are characterized by stable snow cover, bright sunshine, and short days. Severe spells of winter weather with cold winds, extreme temperatures of around −30 °C (−22 °F) and heavy snowfalls are common. Summer starts in June and lasts until August. Summers are usually warm and sunny, with cool evenings and nights. Summers have average temperatures of around 19 °C (66 °F), with extremes of 35 °C (95 °F). Spring and autumn bring fairly mild weather.[[106]](#cite_note-110)
