### Environment

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Turaidas_skats_uz_Gauju_2okt04.JPG/250px-Turaidas_skats_uz_Gauju_2okt04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Turaidas_skats_uz_Gauju_2okt04.JPG)

Latvia has the fifth highest proportion of land covered by forests in the European Union.

Most of the country is composed of fertile lowland plains and moderate hills. In a typical Latvian landscape, a mosaic of vast forests alternates with fields, farmsteads, and pastures. Arable land is spotted with birch groves and wooded clusters, which afford a habitat for numerous plants and animals. Latvia has hundreds of kilometres of undeveloped seashore—lined by pine forests, dunes, and continuous white sand beaches.[[101]](#cite_note-Latvia_in_brief-105)[[109]](#cite_note-Nature_and_Environment_in_Latvia-113)

Latvia has the fifth highest proportion of land covered by forests in the European Union, after Sweden, Finland, Estonia and Slovenia.[[110]](#cite_note-114) Forests account for 3,497,000 ha (8,640,000 acres) or 56% of the total land area.[[99]](#cite_note-Forestry_–_Key_Indicators-103)

Latvia has over 12,500 rivers, which stretch for 38,000 km (24,000 mi). Major rivers include the [Daugava River](/wiki/Daugava_River "Daugava River"), [Lielupe](/wiki/Lielupe "Lielupe"), [Gauja](/wiki/Gauja "Gauja"), [Venta](/wiki/Venta_(river) "Venta (river)"), and [Salaca](/wiki/Salaca "Salaca"), the largest spawning ground for [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") in the eastern Baltic states. There are 2,256 lakes that are bigger than 1 ha (2.5 acres), with a collective area of 1,000 km2 (390 sq mi). [Mires](/wiki/Mire "Mire") occupy 9.9% of Latvia's territory. Of these, 42% are raised bogs; 49% are fens; and 9% are transitional mires. 70% percent of the mires are untouched by civilization, and they are a refuge for many rare species of plants and animals.[[109]](#cite_note-Nature_and_Environment_in_Latvia-113)

Agricultural areas account for 1,815,900 ha (4,487,000 acres) or 29% of the total land area.[[98]](#cite_note-Agriculture_–_Key_Indicators-102) With the dismantling of collective farms, the area devoted to farming decreased dramatically – now farms are predominantly small. Approximately 200 farms, occupying 2,750 ha (6,800 acres), are engaged in ecologically pure farming (using no artificial fertilizers or pesticides).[[109]](#cite_note-Nature_and_Environment_in_Latvia-113)

Latvia's [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") are [Gauja National Park](/wiki/Gauja_National_Park "Gauja National Park") in [Vidzeme](/wiki/Vidzeme "Vidzeme") (since 1973),[[111]](#cite_note-115) [Ķemeri National Park](/wiki/%C4%B6emeri_National_Park "Ķemeri National Park") in [Zemgale](/wiki/Zemgale "Zemgale") (1997), [Slītere National Park](/wiki/Sl%C4%ABtere_National_Park "Slītere National Park") in [Kurzeme](/wiki/Courland "Courland") (1999), and [Rāzna National Park](/wiki/R%C4%81zna_National_Park "Rāzna National Park") in [Latgale](/wiki/Latgale "Latgale") (2007).[[112]](#cite_note-116)

Latvia has a long tradition of conservation. The first laws and regulations were promulgated in the 16th and 17th centuries.[[109]](#cite_note-Nature_and_Environment_in_Latvia-113) There are 706 specially state-level protected natural areas in Latvia: four national parks, one biosphere reserve, 42 nature parks, nine areas of protected landscapes, 260 nature reserves, four strict nature reserves, 355 nature monuments, seven protected marine areas and 24 microreserves.[[113]](#cite_note-117) Nationally protected areas account for 12,790 km2 (4,940 sq mi) or around 20% of Latvia's total land area.[[100]](#cite_note-Geographical_Data_–_Key_Indicators-104) Latvia's Red Book (Endangered Species List of Latvia), which was established in 1977, contains 112 plant species and 119 animal species. Latvia has ratified the international Washington, Bern, and Ramsare conventions.[[109]](#cite_note-Nature_and_Environment_in_Latvia-113)

The 2012 [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index") ranks Latvia second, after Switzerland, based on the environmental performance of the country's policies.[[114]](#cite_note-118)

Access to [biocapacity](/wiki/Biocapacity "Biocapacity") in Latvia is much higher than world average. In 2016, Latvia had 8.5 global hectares[[115]](#cite_note-footprintdata-119) of biocapacity per person within its territory, much more than the world average of 1.6 global hectares per person.[[116]](#cite_note-120) In 2016 Latvia used 6.4 global hectares of biocapacity per person - their [ecological footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") of consumption. This means they use less biocapacity than Latvia contains. As a result, Latvia is running a biocapacity reserve.[[115]](#cite_note-footprintdata-119)

### Biodiversity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/White_wagtail_female_first_summer.jpg/250px-White_wagtail_female_first_summer.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_wagtail_female_first_summer.jpg)

The [white wagtail](/wiki/White_wagtail "White wagtail") is the national bird of Latvia.[[117]](#cite_note-121)

Approximately 30,000 species of flora and fauna have been registered in Latvia.[[118]](#cite_note-122) Larger mammalian wildlife in Latvia include [deer](/wiki/Roe_deer "Roe deer"), [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"), [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose"), [lynx](/wiki/Eurasian_lynx "Eurasian lynx"), [bear](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear"), [fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), [beaver](/wiki/Eurasian_beaver "Eurasian beaver") and [wolves](/wiki/Gray_wolf "Gray wolf").[[119]](#cite_note-123) [Non-marine molluscs of Latvia](/wiki/List_of_non-marine_molluscs_of_Latvia "List of non-marine molluscs of Latvia") include 170 species.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

Species that are endangered in other European countries but common in Latvia include: black stork (*[Ciconia nigra](/wiki/Ciconia_nigra "Ciconia nigra")*), corncrake (*[Crex crex](/wiki/Crex_crex "Crex crex")*), lesser spotted eagle (*[Aquila pomarina](/wiki/Aquila_pomarina "Aquila pomarina")*), white-backed woodpecker (*[Picoides leucotos](/wiki/Picoides_leucotos "Picoides leucotos")*), Eurasian crane (*[Grus grus](/wiki/Grus_grus "Grus grus")*), Eurasian beaver (*[Castor fiber](/wiki/Castor_fiber "Castor fiber")*), Eurasian otter (*[Lutra lutra](/wiki/Lutra_lutra "Lutra lutra")*), European wolf (*[Canis lupus](/wiki/Canis_lupus "Canis lupus")*) and European lynx (*[Felis lynx](/wiki/Eurasian_lynx "Eurasian lynx")*).[[109]](#cite_note-Nature_and_Environment_in_Latvia-113)

[Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), Latvia is shared between the Central European and Northern European provinces of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom"). According to the [WWF](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature"), the territory of Latvia belongs to the [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") of [Sarmatic mixed forests](/wiki/Sarmatic_mixed_forests "Sarmatic mixed forests"). 56 percent[[99]](#cite_note-Forestry_–_Key_Indicators-103) of Latvia's territory is covered by forests, mostly [Scots pine](/wiki/Scots_pine "Scots pine"), [birch](/wiki/Birch "Birch"), and [Norway spruce](/wiki/Norway_spruce "Norway spruce").[[121]](#cite_note-125) It had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 2.09/10, ranking it 159th globally out of 172 countries.[[122]](#cite_note-126)

Several species of flora and fauna are considered national symbols. [Oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") (*[Quercus robur](/wiki/Quercus_robur "Quercus robur")*, [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *ozols*), and [linden](/wiki/Tilia "Tilia") (*[Tilia cordata](/wiki/Tilia_cordata "Tilia cordata")*, [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *liepa*) are Latvia's national trees and the [daisy](/wiki/Leucanthemum_vulgare "Leucanthemum vulgare") (*[Leucanthemum vulgare](/wiki/Leucanthemum_vulgare "Leucanthemum vulgare")*, [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *pīpene*) its national flower. The white wagtail (*[Motacilla alba](/wiki/White_wagtail "White wagtail")*, [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *baltā cielava*) is Latvia's national bird. Its national insect is the [two-spot ladybird](/wiki/Adalia_bipunctata "Adalia bipunctata") (*[Adalia bipunctata](/wiki/Adalia_bipunctata "Adalia bipunctata")*, [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *divpunktu mārīte*). [Amber](/wiki/Amber "Amber"), fossilized tree resin, is one of Latvia's most important cultural symbols. In ancient times, amber found along the Baltic Sea coast was sought by Vikings as well as traders from Egypt, Greece and the Roman Empire. This led to the development of the [Amber Road](/wiki/Amber_Road "Amber Road").[[123]](#cite_note-127)

Several nature reserves protect unspoiled landscapes with a variety of large animals. At [Pape Nature Reserve](/wiki/Pape_Nature_Reserve "Pape Nature Reserve"), where [European bison](/wiki/European_bison "European bison"), wild horses, and [recreated aurochs](/wiki/Heck_cattle "Heck cattle") have been reintroduced, there is now an almost complete [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") [megafauna](/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna") also including moose, deer, and wolf.[[124]](#cite_note-128)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of Latvia](/wiki/Politics_of_Latvia "Politics of Latvia") and [Government of Latvia](/wiki/Government_of_Latvia "Government of Latvia")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  |  |
| [Edgars Rinkēvičs](/wiki/Edgars_Rink%C4%93vi%C4%8Ds "Edgars Rinkēvičs") [President](/wiki/President_of_Latvia "President of Latvia") | [Evika Siliņa](/wiki/Evika_Sili%C5%86a "Evika Siliņa") [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Latvia "Prime Minister of Latvia") |

Politics in Latvia operate under a framework laid out in the [Constitution of Latvia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Latvia "Constitution of Latvia").[[e]](#cite_note-130)

### Government

Main articles: [Parliament of Latvia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Latvia "Parliament of Latvia") and [Cabinet of Latvia](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Latvia "Cabinet of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Flickr_-_Saeima_-_4.maijs_Saeim%C4%81_%286%29.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Saeima_-_4.maijs_Saeim%C4%81_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Saeima_-_4.maijs_Saeim%C4%81_(6).jpg)

The [building](/wiki/House_of_the_Livonian_Noble_Corporation "House of the Livonian Noble Corporation") of the *Saeima*, the parliament of Latvia, in Riga

A [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") parliament, called the *[Saeima](/wiki/Saeima "Saeima"),* is the [legislature](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") of the Republic of Latvia.[[126]](#cite_note-131) The Saeima is also responsible for adopting the [state's budgets](/wiki/Government_budget "Government budget"), approving the state's accounts, appointing and exercising control of the Government, and taking part in international co-operation. [Bills](/wiki/Bill_(proposed_law) "Bill (proposed law)") may be initiated by the Government or by [members of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament").

Latvia is a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") with [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"). Membership of the Saeima is based on [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") of political parties, with a 5% electoral threshold. Latvia elects 100 members to the Saeima. Parliamentary elections are held at least every four years, but it is within the powers of the prime minister to ask the president to call for an election before the term has elapsed. On a [vote of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"), the Saeima may force a single minister or an entire government to resign.[[f]](#cite_note-133)

The Government of Latvia operates as a [cabinet government](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)"), where executive authority is exercised by the prime minister and other [cabinet ministers](/wiki/Cabinet_minister "Cabinet minister"), who head ministries. As the executive branch, the Cabinet is responsible for proposing bills and a budget, executing the laws, and guiding the foreign and internal policies of Latvia. The position of prime minister belongs to the person most likely to command the [confidence](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") of a majority in the Saeima; this is often the current leader of the largest [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") or, more effectively, through a [coalition of parties](/wiki/Political_alliance "Political alliance"). A single party generally does not have sufficient political power in terms of the number of seats to form a cabinet on its own; Saeima has often been ruled by [coalition governments](/wiki/Coalition_governments "Coalition governments"), themselves usually [minority governments](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government") dependent on non-government parties.

The [president](/wiki/President_of_Latvia "President of Latvia") is elected by the *Saeima* in a separate election, also held every four years. The president appoints a prime minister who, together with his cabinet, forms the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") of the government, which has to receive a confidence vote by the *Saeima*. This system also existed before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[128]](#cite_note-134) The most senior civil servants are the thirteen [Secretaries of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State "Secretary of State").[[129]](#cite_note-135)

Following the October [2022 Latvian parliamentary election](/wiki/2022_Latvian_parliamentary_election "2022 Latvian parliamentary election"), Prime Minister [Krišjānis Kariņš](/wiki/Kri%C5%A1j%C4%81nis_Kari%C5%86%C5%A1 "Krišjānis Kariņš") formed the [Second Kariņš cabinet](/wiki/Second_Kari%C5%86%C5%A1_cabinet "Second Kariņš cabinet") in December 2022, a coalition of [New Unity](/wiki/New_Unity "New Unity"), [National Alliance](/wiki/National_Alliance_(Latvia) "National Alliance (Latvia)"), and [United List](/wiki/United_List_(Latvia) "United List (Latvia)").[[130]](#cite_note-136) On 14 August 2023, Kariņš resigned, citing National Alliance's opposition to expanding the coalition to include [The Progressives](/wiki/The_Progressives_(Latvia) "The Progressives (Latvia)") and the [Union of Greens and Farmers](/wiki/Union_of_Greens_and_Farmers "Union of Greens and Farmers").[[131]](#cite_note-137) The [Siliņa cabinet](/wiki/Sili%C5%86a_cabinet "Siliņa cabinet"), comprising New Unity, Union of Greens and Farmers, and The Progressives, was sworn in on 15 September 2023.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Latvia performs in the high range on overall democratic measures, with particular weaknesses in inclusive suffrage, electoral participation, and civic engagement.[[133]](#cite_note-139)[[134]](#cite_note-140)[[135]](#cite_note-141)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Latvia](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Latvia "Administrative divisions of Latvia")

See also: [List of cities in Latvia](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Latvia "List of cities in Latvia"), [Planning regions of Latvia](/wiki/Planning_regions_of_Latvia "Planning regions of Latvia"), [Statistical regions of Latvia](/wiki/Statistical_regions_of_Latvia "Statistical regions of Latvia"), and [Historical regions of Latvia](/wiki/Historical_regions_of_Latvia "Historical regions of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Historical_regions_of_Latvia.svg/250px-Historical_regions_of_Latvia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Historical_regions_of_Latvia.svg)

Historical regions:  
   [Courland](/wiki/Courland "Courland")  
   [Semigallia](/wiki/Semigallia "Semigallia")  
   [Vidzeme](/wiki/Vidzeme "Vidzeme")  
   [Latgale](/wiki/Latgale "Latgale")  
   [Selonia](/wiki/Selonia "Selonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Latvijas_novadi_2021.png/250px-Latvijas_novadi_2021.png)](/wiki/File:Latvijas_novadi_2021.png)

Administrative divisions of Latvia

Latvia is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), currently divided into 43 local government units consisting of 36 municipalities ([Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *novadi*) and 7 state cities ([Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *valstspilsētas*) with their own city council and administration: [Daugavpils](/wiki/Daugavpils "Daugavpils"), [Jelgava](/wiki/Jelgava "Jelgava"), [Jūrmala](/wiki/J%C5%ABrmala "Jūrmala"), [Liepāja](/wiki/Liep%C4%81ja "Liepāja"), [Rēzekne](/wiki/R%C4%93zekne "Rēzekne"), [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), and [Ventspils](/wiki/Ventspils "Ventspils"). There are four [historical and cultural regions in Latvia](/wiki/Cultural_regions_of_Latvia "Cultural regions of Latvia") – [Courland](/wiki/Courland "Courland"), [Latgale](/wiki/Latgale "Latgale"), [Vidzeme](/wiki/Vidzeme "Vidzeme"), [Zemgale](/wiki/Zemgale "Zemgale"), which are recognised in the [Constitution of Latvia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Latvia "Constitution of Latvia"). [Selonia](/wiki/Selonia "Selonia"), a part of Zemgale, is sometimes considered culturally distinct region, but it is not part of any formal division. The borders of historical and cultural regions usually are not explicitly defined and in several sources may vary. In formal divisions, Riga region, which includes the capital and parts of other regions that have a strong relationship with the capital, is also often included in regional divisions; e.g., there are five [planning regions of Latvia](/wiki/Planning_regions_of_Latvia "Planning regions of Latvia") ([Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *plānošanas reģioni*), which were created in 2009 to promote balanced development of all regions. Under this division Riga region includes large parts of what traditionally is considered Vidzeme, Courland, and Zemgale. [Statistical regions of Latvia](/wiki/Statistical_regions_of_Latvia "Statistical regions of Latvia"), established in accordance with the EU [Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics"), duplicate this division.[[136]](#cite_note-142)

The largest city in Latvia is Riga, the second largest city is Daugavpils and the third largest city is Liepaja.

### Political culture

In 2010 parliamentary [election](/wiki/2010_Latvian_parliamentary_election "2010 Latvian parliamentary election") ruling centre-right coalition won 63 out of 100 parliamentary seats. Left-wing opposition [Harmony Centre](/wiki/Harmony_Centre "Harmony Centre") supported by Latvia's Russian-speaking minority got 29 seats.[[137]](#cite_note-143) In November 2013, Latvian Prime Minister [Valdis Dombrovskis](/wiki/Valdis_Dombrovskis "Valdis Dombrovskis"), in office since 2009, resigned after at least 54 people were killed and dozens injured in the [collapse at a supermarket](/wiki/Zolit%C5%ABde_shopping_centre_roof_collapse "Zolitūde shopping centre roof collapse") in Riga.[[138]](#cite_note-144)

In 2014 parliamentary [election](/wiki/2014_Latvian_parliamentary_election "2014 Latvian parliamentary election") was won again by the ruling centre-right coalition formed by the [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(Latvia) "Unity (Latvia)") Party, the [National Alliance](/wiki/National_Alliance_(Latvia) "National Alliance (Latvia)") and the [Union of Greens and Farmers](/wiki/Union_of_Greens_and_Farmers "Union of Greens and Farmers"). They got 61 seats and Harmony got 24.[[139]](#cite_note-145) In December 2015, country's first female prime minister, in office since January 2014, [Laimdota Straujuma](/wiki/Laimdota_Straujuma "Laimdota Straujuma") resigned.[[140]](#cite_note-146) In February 2016, a coalition of Union of Greens and Farmers, The Unity and National Alliance was formed by new Prime Minister [Maris Kucinskis](/wiki/Maris_Kucinskis "Maris Kucinskis").[[141]](#cite_note-147)

In 2018 parliamentary [election](/wiki/2018_Latvian_parliamentary_election "2018 Latvian parliamentary election") pro-Russian Harmony was again the biggest party securing 23 out of 100 seats, the second and third were the new populist parties [KPV LV](/wiki/KPV_LV "KPV LV") and [New Conservative Party](/wiki/New_Conservative_Party_(Latvia) "New Conservative Party (Latvia)"). Ruling coalition, comprising the Union of Greens and Farmers, the National Alliance and the Unity party, lost.[[142]](#cite_note-148) In January 2019, Latvia got a government led by new Prime Minister [Krisjanis Karins](/wiki/Krisjanis_Karins "Krisjanis Karins") of the centre-right [New Unity](/wiki/New_Unity_(Latvian_political_party) "New Unity (Latvian political party)"). Karins' coalition was formed by five of the seven parties in parliament, excluding only the pro-Russia Harmony party and the Union of Greens and Farmers.[[143]](#cite_note-149) On 15 September 2023, [Evika Siliņa](/wiki/Evika_Sili%C5%86a "Evika Siliņa") became the new prime minister of Latvia, following resignation of former Prime Minister Krišjānis Kariņš previous month. Siliņa's government is a three-party coalition between her own [New Unity](/wiki/New_Unity "New Unity") (JV) party, the Greens and Farmers Union (ZZS), and the social-democratic Progressives (PRO) with total 52 of 100 seats in the parliament.[[144]](#cite_note-150)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Latvia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Latvia "Foreign relations of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Ministerio_de_Asuntos_Exteriores%2C_Riga%2C_Letonia%2C_2012-08-07%2C_DD_01.JPG/250px-Ministerio_de_Asuntos_Exteriores%2C_Riga%2C_Letonia%2C_2012-08-07%2C_DD_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ministerio_de_Asuntos_Exteriores,_Riga,_Letonia,_2012-08-07,_DD_01.JPG)

The building of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Riga

Latvia is a member of the United Nations, [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe"), [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), and [WTO](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"). It is also a member of the [Council of the Baltic Sea States](/wiki/Council_of_the_Baltic_Sea_States "Council of the Baltic Sea States") and [Nordic Investment Bank](/wiki/Nordic_Investment_Bank "Nordic Investment Bank"). It was a member of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") (1921–1946). Latvia is part of the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area")[[145]](#cite_note-151) and joined the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") on 1 January 2014.[[146]](#cite_note-152)

Latvia has established diplomatic relations with 158 countries. It has 44 diplomatic and consular missions and maintains 34 embassies and 9 permanent representations abroad. There are 37 foreign embassies and 11 international organisations in Latvia's capital [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"). Latvia hosts one European Union institution, the [Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications](/wiki/Body_of_European_Regulators_of_Electronic_Communications "Body of European Regulators of Electronic Communications") (BEREC).[[147]](#cite_note-153)

Latvia's foreign policy priorities include co-operation in the Baltic Sea region, European integration, active involvement in international organisations, contribution to European and transatlantic security and defence structures, participation in international civilian and military peacekeeping operations, and development co-operation, particularly the strengthening of stability and democracy in the EU's [Eastern Partnership](/wiki/Eastern_Partnership "Eastern Partnership") countries.[[148]](#cite_note-154)[[149]](#cite_note-155)[[150]](#cite_note-156)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Foreign_Ministers_of_Nordic_and_Baltic_countries_met_in_Helsinki%2C_30.08.2011_%28Photographer_Eero_Kuosmanen%29.jpg/250px-Foreign_Ministers_of_Nordic_and_Baltic_countries_met_in_Helsinki%2C_30.08.2011_%28Photographer_Eero_Kuosmanen%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foreign_Ministers_of_Nordic_and_Baltic_countries_met_in_Helsinki,_30.08.2011_(Photographer_Eero_Kuosmanen).jpg)

Foreign ministers of the Nordic and Baltic countries in Helsinki, 2011

Since the early 1990s, Latvia has been involved in active trilateral [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") co-operation with its neighbours [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), and Nordic-Baltic co-operation with the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"). Latvia is a member of the interparliamentary [Baltic Assembly](/wiki/Baltic_Assembly "Baltic Assembly"), the intergovernmental [Baltic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Baltic_Council_of_Ministers "Baltic Council of Ministers") and the [Council of the Baltic Sea States](/wiki/Council_of_the_Baltic_Sea_States "Council of the Baltic Sea States").[[151]](#cite_note-157) [Nordic-Baltic Eight](/wiki/Nordic-Baltic_Eight "Nordic-Baltic Eight") (NB-8) is the joint co-operation of the governments of Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Iceland, Latvia, Lithuania, Norway, and Sweden.[[152]](#cite_note-Co-operation_of_Baltic_and_Nordic_States-158) Nordic-Baltic Six (NB-6), comprising Nordic-Baltic countries that are European Union member states, is a framework for meetings on EU-related issues. Interparliamentary co-operation between the Baltic Assembly and [Nordic Council](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council") was signed in 1992 and since 2006 annual meetings are held as well as regular meetings on other levels.[[152]](#cite_note-Co-operation_of_Baltic_and_Nordic_States-158) Joint Nordic-Baltic co-operation initiatives include the education programme NordPlus[[153]](#cite_note-159) and mobility programmes for public administration,[[154]](#cite_note-160) business and industry[[155]](#cite_note-161) and culture.[[156]](#cite_note-162) The [Nordic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Nordic_Council_of_Ministers "Nordic Council of Ministers") has an office in Riga.[[157]](#cite_note-163)

Latvia participates in the [Northern Dimension](/wiki/Northern_Dimension "Northern Dimension") and [Baltic Sea Region Programme](/wiki/Baltic_Sea_Region_Programme "Baltic Sea Region Programme"), European Union initiatives to foster cross-border co-operation in the Baltic Sea region and Northern Europe. The secretariat of the Northern Dimension Partnership on Culture (NDPC) will be located in Riga.[[158]](#cite_note-164) In 2013 Riga hosted the annual [Northern Future Forum](/wiki/Northern_Future_Forum "Northern Future Forum"), a two-day informal meeting of the prime ministers of the Nordic-Baltic countries and the UK.[[159]](#cite_note-165) The Enhanced Partnership in Northern Europe or *e-Pine* is the [U.S. Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") diplomatic framework for co-operation with the Nordic-Baltic countries.[[160]](#cite_note-166)

Latvia hosted the [2006 NATO Summit](/wiki/2006_Riga_summit "2006 Riga summit") and since then the annual Riga Conference has become a leading foreign and security policy forum in Northern Europe.[[161]](#cite_note-167) Latvia held the [Presidency of the Council of the European Union](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union") in the first half of 2015.[[162]](#cite_note-168)

Since February 2022 Latvia's relations with Russia have deteriorated to the extent that Latvia withdrew its ambassador from Russia and expelled Russia's ambassador to Latvia in January 2023[[163]](#cite_note-169) and banned Russians from entering Latvia.

### Military

Main article: [Military of Latvia](/wiki/Military_of_Latvia "Military of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Naval_visit%2C_Belfast_%283%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_667223.jpg/250px-Naval_visit%2C_Belfast_%283%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_667223.jpg)](/wiki/File:Naval_visit,_Belfast_(3)_-_geograph.org.uk_-_667223.jpg)

Naval Forces minehunter *Imanta*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/CVR%28T%29_Scimitar_In_Latvia.jpg/250px-CVR%28T%29_Scimitar_In_Latvia.jpg)](/wiki/File:CVR(T)_Scimitar_In_Latvia.jpg)

[CVR(T) Scimitar](/wiki/Combat_Vehicle_Reconnaissance_(Tracked) "Combat Vehicle Reconnaissance (Tracked)") in Latvian service

The [National Armed Forces](/wiki/Latvian_National_Armed_Forces "Latvian National Armed Forces") (Latvian: *Nacionālie bruņotie spēki (NAF)*) of Latvia consists of the [Land Forces](/wiki/Latvian_Land_Forces "Latvian Land Forces"), [Naval Forces](/wiki/Latvian_Naval_Forces "Latvian Naval Forces"), [Air Force](/wiki/Latvian_Air_Force "Latvian Air Force"), [National Guard](/wiki/Latvian_National_Guard "Latvian National Guard"), [Special Tasks Unit](/wiki/Latvian_Special_Tasks_Unit "Latvian Special Tasks Unit"), [Military Police](/wiki/Latvian_Military_Police "Latvian Military Police"), [NAF staff Battalion](/wiki/Latvian_National_Armed_Forces_Staff_Battalion "Latvian National Armed Forces Staff Battalion"), Training and Doctrine Command, and Logistics Command. Latvia's defence concept is based upon the Swedish-Finnish model of a rapid response force composed of a mobilisation base and a small group of career professionals. From 1 January 2007, Latvia switched to a professional fully contract-based army.[[164]](#cite_note-170)

Latvia participates in international peacekeeping and security operations. Latvian armed forces have contributed to [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and EU military operations in Bosnia and Herzegovina (1996–2009), Albania (1999), Kosovo (2000–2009), Macedonia (2003), Iraq (2005–2006), Afghanistan (since 2003), Somalia (since 2011) and Mali (since 2013).[[165]](#cite_note-Participation_in_International_operations-171)[[166]](#cite_note-Previous_operations-172)[[167]](#cite_note-173) Latvia also took part in the US-led [Multi-National Force](/wiki/Multi-National_Force_%E2%80%93_Iraq "Multi-National Force – Iraq") operation in Iraq (2003–2008)[[168]](#cite_note-174) and [OSCE](/wiki/OSCE "OSCE") missions in Georgia, Kosovo and Macedonia.[[169]](#cite_note-175) Latvian armed forces contributed to a UK-led [Battlegroup](/wiki/EU_Battlegroup "EU Battlegroup") in 2013 and the [Nordic Battlegroup](/wiki/Nordic_Battle_Group "Nordic Battle Group") in 2015 under the [Common Security and Defence Policy](/wiki/Common_Security_and_Defence_Policy "Common Security and Defence Policy") (CSDP) of the European Union.[[170]](#cite_note-176) Latvia acts as the lead nation in the coordination of the [Northern Distribution Network](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present)#Northern_Distribution_Network "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)") for transportation of non-lethal [ISAF](/wiki/ISAF "ISAF") cargo by air and rail to Afghanistan.[[171]](#cite_note-177)[[172]](#cite_note-178)[[173]](#cite_note-179) It is part of the Nordic Transition Support Unit (NTSU), which renders joint force contributions in support of Afghan security structures ahead of the withdrawal of Nordic and Baltic ISAF forces in 2014.[[174]](#cite_note-180) Since 1996 more than 3600 military personnel have participated in international operations,[[166]](#cite_note-Previous_operations-172) of whom 7 soldiers perished.[[175]](#cite_note-181) Per capita, Latvia is one of the largest contributors to international military operations.[[176]](#cite_note-182)

Latvian civilian experts have contributed to EU civilian missions: border assistance mission to Moldova and Ukraine (2005–2009), rule of law missions in Iraq (2006 and 2007) and Kosovo (since 2008), police mission in Afghanistan (since 2007) and monitoring mission in Georgia (since 2008).[[165]](#cite_note-Participation_in_International_operations-171)

Since March 2004, when the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") joined NATO, fighter jets of NATO members have been deployed on a rotational basis for the [Baltic Air Policing](/wiki/Baltic_Air_Policing "Baltic Air Policing") mission at [Šiauliai Airport](/wiki/%C5%A0iauliai_International_Airport "Šiauliai International Airport") in Lithuania to guard the Baltic airspace. Latvia participates in several [NATO Centres of Excellence](/wiki/NATO_Centres_of_Excellence "NATO Centres of Excellence"): Civil-Military Co-operation in the Netherlands, [Cooperative Cyber Defence](/wiki/Cooperative_Cyber_Defence_Centre_of_Excellence "Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence") in Estonia and Energy Security in Lithuania. It plans to establish the NATO Strategic Communications Centre of Excellence in Riga.[[177]](#cite_note-183)

Latvia co-operates with Estonia and Lithuania in several trilateral Baltic defence co-operation initiatives:

* Baltic Battalion *(BALTBAT)* – infantry battalion for participation in international peace support operations, headquartered near [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), Latvia;
* [Baltic Naval Squadron](/wiki/Baltic_Naval_Squadron "Baltic Naval Squadron") *(BALTRON)* – naval force with mine countermeasures capabilities, headquartered near [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), Estonia;
* [Baltic Air Surveillance Network](/wiki/Baltic_Air_Surveillance_Network "Baltic Air Surveillance Network") *(BALTNET)* – air surveillance information system, headquartered near [Kaunas](/wiki/Kaunas "Kaunas"), Lithuania;
* Joint military educational institutions: [Baltic Defence College](/wiki/Baltic_Defence_College "Baltic Defence College") in [Tartu](/wiki/Tartu "Tartu"), Estonia, Baltic Diving Training Centre in [Liepāja](/wiki/Liep%C4%81ja "Liepāja"), Latvia and Baltic Naval Communications Training Centre in [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), Estonia.[[178]](#cite_note-184)

Future co-operation will include sharing of national infrastructures for training purposes and specialisation of training areas *(BALTTRAIN)* and collective formation of battalion-sized contingents for use in the NATO rapid-response force.[[179]](#cite_note-185) In January 2011, the Baltic states were invited to join [Nordic Defence Cooperation](/wiki/Nordic_Defence_Cooperation "Nordic Defence Cooperation"), the defence framework of the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries").[[180]](#cite_note-186) In November 2012, the three countries agreed to create a joint military staff in 2013.[[181]](#cite_note-187)

On 21 April 2022, Latvian Saeima passed amendments developed by the Ministry of Defence for the legislative draft Amendments to the Law on Financing of National Defence, which provide for gradual increase in the defence budget to 2.5% of the country's GDP over the course of the next three year.[[182]](#cite_note-188)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Latvia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Latvia "Human rights in Latvia")

See also: [LGBT rights in Latvia](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Latvia "LGBT rights in Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Europride_2015.JPG/250px-Europride_2015.JPG)](/wiki/File:Europride_2015.JPG)

[Europride](/wiki/Europride "Europride") 2015 in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga")

According to the reports by [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") and the [US Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State"), human rights in Latvia are generally respected by the government:[[183]](#cite_note-departmentofstate1-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190) Latvia is ranked above-average among the world's sovereign states in democracy,[[185]](#cite_note-191) [press freedom](/wiki/Press_freedom "Press freedom"),[[186]](#cite_note-192) [privacy](/wiki/Privacy "Privacy")[[187]](#cite_note-193) and [human development](/wiki/Human_development_(humanity) "Human development (humanity)").[[188]](#cite_note-194)

More than 56% of leading positions are held by women in Latvia, which ranks first in Europe; Latvia ranks first in the world in women's rights sharing the position with five other European countries according to [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank").[[189]](#cite_note-195)

The country has a large [ethnic Russian](/wiki/Russians_in_Latvia "Russians in Latvia") community, which was guaranteed basic rights under the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Latvia "Constitution of Latvia") and [international human rights laws](/wiki/International_human_rights_law "International human rights law") ratified by the Latvian government.[[183]](#cite_note-departmentofstate1-189)[[190]](#cite_note-196)

Approximately 206,000 [non-citizens](/wiki/Non-citizens_(Latvia) "Non-citizens (Latvia)")[[191]](#cite_note-197) – including [stateless persons](/wiki/Stateless_persons "Stateless persons") – have limited access to some political rights – only citizens are allowed to participate in parliamentary or municipal elections, although there are no limitations in regards to joining political parties or other political organizations.[[192]](#cite_note-198)[[193]](#cite_note-199) In 2011, the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") [High Commissioner on National Minorities](/wiki/High_Commissioner_on_National_Minorities "High Commissioner on National Minorities") "urged Latvia to allow non-citizens to vote in municipal elections."[[194]](#cite_note-200) Additionally, there have been reports of police abuse of [detainees](/wiki/Detainee "Detainee") and arrestees, poor prison conditions and overcrowding, judicial corruption, incidents of violence against [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities "Ethnic minorities"), and societal violence and incidents of government discrimination against homosexuals.[[183]](#cite_note-departmentofstate1-189)[[195]](#cite_note-201)[[196]](#cite_note-202) [Same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") is constitutionally prohibited in Latvia, but [same-sex civil unions](/wiki/Recognition_of_same-sex_unions_in_Latvia "Recognition of same-sex unions in Latvia") are recognized.[[197]](#cite_note-203)[[198]](#cite_note-204)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Latvia](/wiki/Economy_of_Latvia "Economy of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/BlueEurozone.svg/250px-BlueEurozone.svg.png)](/wiki/File:BlueEurozone.svg)

Latvia is part of   
  +   [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market")

[Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone")

[Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") (not shown).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/GDP_per_capita_Baltics.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_Baltics.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_Baltics.svg)

Real GPD per capita development of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania

Latvia is a member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (1999) and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (2004). On 1 January 2014, the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") became the country's currency, superseding the [Lats](/wiki/Latvian_lats "Latvian lats"). According to statistics in late 2013, 45% of the population supported the introduction of the euro, while 52% opposed it.[[199]](#cite_note-205) Following the introduction of the Euro, Eurobarometer surveys in January 2014 showed support for the euro to be around 53%, close to the European average.[[200]](#cite_note-206)

Since the year 2000, Latvia has had one of the highest (GDP) growth rates in Europe.[[201]](#cite_note-207) However, the chiefly consumption-driven growth in Latvia resulted in the collapse of Latvian GDP in late 2008 and early 2009, exacerbated by the global economic crisis, shortage of credit and huge money resources used for the bailout of [Parex Bank](/wiki/Parex_Bank "Parex Bank").[[202]](#cite_note-208) The Latvian economy fell 18% in the first three months of 2009, the biggest fall in the European Union.[[203]](#cite_note-209)[[204]](#cite_note-210)

The economic crisis of 2009 proved earlier assumptions that the fast-growing economy was heading for implosion of the [economic bubble](/wiki/Economic_bubble "Economic bubble"), because it was driven mainly by growth of domestic [consumption](/wiki/Consumption_(economics) "Consumption (economics)"), financed by a serious increase of private [debt](/wiki/Debt "Debt"), as well as a negative foreign [trade balance](/wiki/Balance_of_trade "Balance of trade"). The prices of real estate, which rose 150% from 2004 to 2006, was a significant contributor to the economic bubble.[[205]](#cite_note-211)

[Privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation") in Latvia is almost complete. Virtually all of the previously state-owned small and medium companies have been privatised, leaving only a small number of politically sensitive large state companies. The private sector accounted for 70% of the country's GDP in 2006.
[[206]](#cite_note-212)

Foreign investment in Latvia is still modest compared with the levels in north-central Europe. A law expanding the scope for selling land, including to foreigners, was passed in 1997. Representing 10.2% of Latvia's total foreign direct investment, American companies invested $127 million in 1999. In the same year, the United States of America exported $58.2 million of goods and services to Latvia and imported $87.9 million. Eager to join Western economic institutions like the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), Latvia signed a Europe Agreement with the EU in 1995—with a 4-year transition period. Latvia and the United States have signed treaties on investment, trade, and intellectual property protection and avoidance of double taxation.[[207]](#cite_note-213)[[208]](#cite_note-214)

In 2010, Latvia launched a Residence by Investment program (Golden Visa) in order to attract foreign investors and make local economy benefit from it. This program allows investors to get a Latvian residence permit by investing at least €250,000 in property or in an enterprise with at least 50 employees and an annual turnover of at least €10M.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Economic contraction and recovery (2008–12)

Main article: [2008–2010 Latvian financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932010_Latvian_financial_crisis "2008–2010 Latvian financial crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/AirBaltic_Boeing_757-200_at_RIX.jpg/250px-AirBaltic_Boeing_757-200_at_RIX.jpg)](/wiki/File:AirBaltic_Boeing_757-200_at_RIX.jpg)

An [airBaltic](/wiki/AirBaltic "AirBaltic") Boeing 757−200WL takes off at [Riga International Airport](/wiki/Riga_International_Airport "Riga International Airport") (RIX).

The Latvian economy entered a phase of fiscal contraction during the second half of 2008 after an extended period of credit-based speculation and unrealistic appreciation in real estate values. The national account deficit for 2007, for example, represented more than 22% of the GDP for the year while inflation was running at 10%.[[209]](#cite_note-cia-215)

Latvia's unemployment rate rose sharply in this period from a low of 5.4% in November 2007 to over 22%.[[210]](#cite_note-216) In April 2010 Latvia had the highest unemployment rate in the EU, at 22.5%, ahead of Spain, which had 19.7%.[[211]](#cite_note-217)

[Paul Krugman](/wiki/Paul_Krugman "Paul Krugman"), the Nobel Laureate in economics for 2008, wrote in his [New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") Op-Ed column on 15 December 2008:

> The most acute problems are on Europe's periphery, where many smaller economies are experiencing crises strongly reminiscent of past crises in Latin America and Asia: Latvia is the new Argentina[[212]](#cite_note-218)

However, by 2010, commentators[[213]](#cite_note-economist-219)[[214]](#cite_note-220) noted signs of stabilisation in the Latvian economy. Rating agency [Standard & Poor's](/wiki/Standard_%26_Poor%27s "Standard & Poor's") raised its outlook on Latvia's debt from negative to stable.[[213]](#cite_note-economist-219) Latvia's current account, which had been in deficit by 27% in late 2006 was in surplus in February 2010.[[213]](#cite_note-economist-219) Kenneth Orchard, senior analyst at [Moody's Investors Service](/wiki/Moody%27s_Investors_Service "Moody's Investors Service") argued that:

> The strengthening regional economy is supporting Latvian production and exports, while the sharp swing in the current account balance suggests that the country's 'internal devaluation' is working.[[215]](#cite_note-221)

The [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF") concluded the First Post-Program Monitoring Discussions with the Republic of Latvia in July 2012 announcing that Latvia's economy has been recovering strongly since 2010, following the deep downturn in 2008–09. Real GDP growth of 5.5 percent in 2011 was underpinned by export growth and a recovery in domestic demand. The growth momentum has continued into 2012 and 2013 despite deteriorating external conditions, and the economy is expected to expand by 4.1 percent in 2014. The unemployment rate has receded from its peak of more than 20 percent in 2010 to around 9.3 percent in 2014.[[216]](#cite_note-222)

Economic recovery

GDP at current prices rose from €23.7 billion in 2014 to €30.5 billion in 2019. The employment rate rose in the same period from 59.1% to 65% with unemployment falling from 10.8% to 6.5%.[[217]](#cite_note-223)

### Infrastructure

#### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Latvia](/wiki/Transport_in_Latvia "Transport in Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Ventspils_osta.jpg/250px-Ventspils_osta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ventspils_osta.jpg)

The [Port of Ventspils](/wiki/Free_port_of_Ventspils "Free port of Ventspils") is one of the busiest ports in the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states").

The transport sector is around 14% of GDP. Transit between Russia, Belarus, Kazakhstan as well as other Asian countries and the West used to be large.[[218]](#cite_note-wpt-224)

The four biggest ports of Latvia are located in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), [Ventspils](/wiki/Ventspils "Ventspils"), [Liepāja](/wiki/Liep%C4%81ja "Liepāja") and [Skulte](/wiki/Saulkrasti "Saulkrasti"). Most transit traffic uses these and half the cargo is crude oil and oil products.[[218]](#cite_note-wpt-224) [Free port of Ventspils](/wiki/Free_port_of_Ventspils "Free port of Ventspils") is one of the busiest ports in the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"). Apart from road and railway connections, Ventspils is also linked to oil extraction fields and prior to 2022, transportation routes of Russian Federation via system of two pipelines from [Polotsk](/wiki/Polotsk "Polotsk"), Belarus.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Riga International Airport](/wiki/Riga_International_Airport "Riga International Airport") is the busiest airport in the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") with 7.8 million passengers in 2019. It has direct flight to over 80 destinations in 30 countries. The only other airport handling regular commercial flights is [Liepāja International Airport](/wiki/Liep%C4%81ja_International_Airport "Liepāja International Airport").
[airBaltic](/wiki/AirBaltic "AirBaltic") is the Latvian flag carrier airline and a low-cost carrier with hubs in all three [Baltic States](/wiki/Baltic_States "Baltic States"), but main base in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), Latvia.[[219]](#cite_note-225)

[Latvian Railway](/wiki/Latvian_Railways "Latvian Railways")'s main network consists of 1,860 km of which 1,826 km is 1,520 mm [Russian gauge](/wiki/Russian_gauge "Russian gauge") railway of which 251 km are electrified, making it the longest railway network in the [Baltic States](/wiki/Baltic_States "Baltic States"). Latvia's railway network is currently incompatible with European [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") lines.[[220]](#cite_note-226) However, [Rail Baltica](/wiki/Rail_Baltica "Rail Baltica") railway, linking Helsinki-Tallinn-Riga-Kaunas-Warsaw is under construction and is set to be completed in 2026.[[221]](#cite_note-227)

National road network in Latvia totals 1675 km of main roads, 5473 km of regional roads and 13 064 km of local roads. Municipal roads in Latvia totals 30 439 km of roads and 8039 km of streets.[[222]](#cite_note-228) The best known roads are [A1](/wiki/Via_Baltica "Via Baltica") ([European route E67](/wiki/European_route_E67 "European route E67")), connecting [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") and [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), as well as [European route E22](/wiki/European_route_E22 "European route E22"), connecting [Ventspils](/wiki/Ventspils "Ventspils") and [Terehova](/wiki/Terehova,_Latvia "Terehova, Latvia"). In 2017 there were a total of 803,546 licensed vehicles in Latvia.[[223]](#cite_note-229)

#### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Latvia](/wiki/Energy_in_Latvia "Energy in Latvia")

Latvia has three large hydroelectric power stations in [Pļaviņu HES](/wiki/P%C4%BCavi%C5%86u_HES "Pļaviņu HES") (908 MW), [Rīgas HES](/wiki/R%C4%ABgas_HES "Rīgas HES") (402 MW) and [Ķeguma HES-2](/wiki/%C4%B6eguma_HES-2 "Ķeguma HES-2") (248 MW).[[224]](#cite_note-230) In recent years a couple of dozen of wind farms as well as biogas or biomass power stations of different scale have been built in Latvia.[[225]](#cite_note-231) In 2022, the Latvian Prime Minister announced about the planned investments of 1 billion euros in the new [wind farms](/wiki/Wind_farm "Wind farm") and the completed project will expectedly provide additional 800 MW of capacity.[[226]](#cite_note-232)

Latvia operates *Inčukalns underground gas storage facility*, one of the largest [underground gas storage facilities](/wiki/Natural_gas_storage "Natural gas storage") in Europe and the only one in the Baltic states. Unique geological conditions at Inčukalns and other locations in Latvia are particularly suitable for underground gas storage.[[227]](#cite_note-233)

The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) in 2023 was estimated to be 1.36 children born/woman, which is lower than the replacement rate of 2.1. In 2012, 45.0% of births were to unmarried women.[[228]](#cite_note-234) The life expectancy in 2013 was estimated at 73.2 years (68.1 years male, 78.5 years female).[[209]](#cite_note-cia-215) As of 2015, Latvia is estimated to have the lowest male-to-female ratio in the world, at 0.85 males per female.[[229]](#cite_note-235) In 2017, there were 1,054,433 females and 895,683 males living in Latvian territory. Every year, more boys are born than girls. Up to the age of 39, there are more males than females. Above the age of 70, there are 2.3 times as many females as males.

### Ethnic groups

In 2025, [Latvians](/wiki/Latvian_people "Latvian people") formed about 65.5% of the population, while [Russians](/wiki/Russians_in_the_Baltic_states "Russians in the Baltic states") were 24.1%, [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusians "Belarusians") 2.9%, [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") 2.9%, [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") 1.9%, [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians") 1.1%, and other ethnic groups 1.6%.[[g]](#cite_note-236)[[230]](#cite_note-237)

In some cities, including [Daugavpils](/wiki/Daugavpils "Daugavpils") and [Rēzekne](/wiki/R%C4%93zekne "Rēzekne"), ethnic Latvians constitute a minority of the total population. Despite a steadily increasing proportion of ethnic Latvians for more than a decade, ethnic Latvians also still make up slightly less than a half of the population of the capital city of Latvia – [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The share of ethnic Latvians declined from 77% (1,467,035) in 1935 to 52% (1,387,757) in 1989.[[231]](#cite_note-238) In the context of a decreasing overall population, there were fewer Latvians in 2011 than in 1989, but their share of the population was larger – 1,285,136 (62.1% of the population).[[232]](#cite_note-239)

The majority of Latvia's population are Latvians, who are an ethnic Baltic people. The country also has a significant Russian minority, as well as smaller populations of Ukrainians, Belarusians, and other Slavic peoples. These ethnic groups are all descended from peoples who settled in Latvia during the centuries of Russian and Soviet rule.

Latvia's ethnic diversity is a result of a number of factors, including a long history of foreign rule, its location on the Baltic Sea trade route, and its proximity to other Slavic countries. The Russian Empire conquered Latvia in the 18th century and ruled the country for over 200 years. During this time, the Russian authorities encouraged the settlement of Russian colonists in Latvia. After the collapse of the Russian Empire in 1918, Latvia became an independent country. However, the country was occupied by the Soviet Union in 1940 and remained under Soviet rule until 1991. The Soviets expelled some groups and resettled others in Latvia, especially Russians. After 1991 many of the expellees returned to Latvia.[[233]](#cite_note-240)

As a result of deteriorating relations with Russia, Latvia has decided it does not want Russian citizens in Latvia who will not integrate. In late 2023 it is expected that around 5,000–6,000 Russians will be returned to Russia as they have made little effort to learn the Latvian language, integrate with Latvia, or apply to become Latvian citizens.[[234]](#cite_note-241)

### Language

Further information: [Language policy in Latvia](/wiki/Language_policy_in_Latvia "Language policy in Latvia")

The sole official language of Latvia is [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"), which belongs to the [Baltic language sub-group](/wiki/Baltic_languages "Baltic languages") of the [Balto-Slavic branch](/wiki/Balto-Slavic_languages "Balto-Slavic languages") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Another notable language of Latvia is the nearly extinct [Livonian language](/wiki/Livonian_language "Livonian language") of the [Finnic](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") branch of the [Uralic language family](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages"), which enjoys protection by law; [Latgalian](/wiki/Latgalian_language "Latgalian language") – as a dialect of Latvian is also protected by Latvian law but as a historical variation of the Latvian language. [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), which was widely spoken during the Soviet period, is still the most widely used minority language by far (in 2023, 37.7% spoke it as their mother tongue and 34.6% spoke it at home, including people who were not ethnically Russian).[[21]](#cite_note-eng.lsm.lv-24) While it is now required that all school students learn Latvian, schools also include English, German, French and Russian in their curricula. English is also widely accepted in Latvia in business and tourism. As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Latvia&action=edit) there were 109 schools for minorities that use Russian as the language of instruction (27% of all students) for 40% of subjects (the remaining 60% of subjects are taught in Latvian).

On 18 February 2012, Latvia held a [constitutional referendum](/wiki/2012_Latvian_constitutional_referendum "2012 Latvian constitutional referendum") on whether to adopt Russian as a second official language.[[235]](#cite_note-242) According to the Central Election Commission, 74.8% voted against, 24.9% voted for and the voter turnout was 71.1%.[[236]](#cite_note-243)

From 2019, instruction in the [Russian language](/wiki/Russian_language_in_Latvia "Russian language in Latvia") was gradually discontinued in private colleges and [universities](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") in Latvia, as well as general instruction in Latvian public [high schools](/wiki/High_school "High school"),[[237]](#cite_note-244)[[238]](#cite_note-245) except for subjects related to culture and history of the Russian minority, such as Russian language and literature classes.[[239]](#cite_note-246) All schools, including pre-schools, still using the Russian language in 2023 need to transition to using Latvian in all classes within three years.[[240]](#cite_note-247)

The largest religion in Latvia is [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (79%).[[209]](#cite_note-cia-215)[[241]](#cite_note-Tieslietu_Ministrija-248) The largest groups as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Latvia&action=edit) were:

* [Evangelical Lutheran Church of Latvia](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Latvia "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Latvia") – 708,773[[241]](#cite_note-Tieslietu_Ministrija-248)
* [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Latvia "Catholic Church in Latvia") – 500,000[[241]](#cite_note-Tieslietu_Ministrija-248)
* [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") – 370,000[[241]](#cite_note-Tieslietu_Ministrija-248)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Riga_Dom_02.JPG/250px-Riga_Dom_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Riga_Dom_02.JPG)

[Riga Cathedral](/wiki/Riga_Cathedral "Riga Cathedral")

In the [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") Poll 2010, 38% of Latvian citizens responded that "they believe there is a God", while 48% answered that "they believe there is some sort of spirit or life force" and 11% stated that "they do not believe there is any sort of spirit, God, or life force".

Lutheranism was more prominent before the Soviet occupation, when it was adhered to by about 60% of the population, a reflection of the country's strong historical links with the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), and to the influence of the [Hansa](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") in particular and Germany in general. Since then, Lutheranism has declined to a slightly greater extent than Catholicism in all three [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"). The Evangelical Lutheran Church, with an estimated 600,000 members in 1956, was affected most adversely. An internal document of 18 March 1987, near the end of communist rule, spoke of an active membership that had shrunk to only 25,000 in Latvia, but the faith has since experienced a revival.[[242]](#cite_note-249)

The country's Orthodox Christians belong to the [Latvian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Latvian_Orthodox_Church "Latvian Orthodox Church"), a semi-autonomous body within the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"). In 2011, there were 416 religious [Jews in Latvia](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Latvia "History of the Jews in Latvia") and 319 [Muslims in Latvia](/wiki/Islam_in_Latvia "Islam in Latvia").[[241]](#cite_note-Tieslietu_Ministrija-248) As of 2004, there were more than 600 Latvian [neopagans](/wiki/Neopaganism "Neopaganism"), *[Dievturi](/wiki/Dievtur%C4%ABba "Dievturība")* (The Godskeepers), whose religion is based on [Latvian mythology](/wiki/Latvian_mythology "Latvian mythology").[[243]](#cite_note-250)[[244]](#cite_note-251) About 21% of the total population is not affiliated with a specific religion.[[241]](#cite_note-Tieslietu_Ministrija-248)

Latvia has been seeking for a number of years to separate the Latvian Orthodox Church from Moscow, stating that longstanding ties to Russia pose "national security concerns".[[245]](#cite_note-252) This was achieved in September 2022 with a law removing all influence or power over the Orthodox Church from non-Latvians, which would include the patriarch of Moscow.[[246]](#cite_note-253)

### Education and science

Main article: [Education in Latvia](/wiki/Education_in_Latvia "Education in Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Latvijas_Universit%C4%81te_-_ogre11_-_Panoramio.jpg/330px-Latvijas_Universit%C4%81te_-_ogre11_-_Panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Latvijas_Universit%C4%81te_-_ogre11_-_Panoramio.jpg)

University of Latvia

The [University of Latvia](/wiki/University_of_Latvia "University of Latvia") and [Riga Technical University](/wiki/Riga_Technical_University "Riga Technical University") are two major universities in the country, both successors to Riga Polytechnical Institute, and located in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga").[[247]](#cite_note-254) Other important universities, which were established on the base of State University of Latvia, include the [Latvia University of Life Sciences and Technologies](/wiki/Latvia_University_of_Life_Sciences_and_Technologies "Latvia University of Life Sciences and Technologies") (established in 1939 on the basis of the Faculty of Agriculture) and [Riga Stradiņš University](/wiki/Riga_Stradi%C5%86%C5%A1_University "Riga Stradiņš University") (established in 1950 on the basis of the Faculty of Medicine). Both nowadays cover a variety of different fields. The [University of Daugavpils](/wiki/University_of_Daugavpils "University of Daugavpils") is another significant centre of education.

Latvia closed 131 schools between 2006 and 2010, which is a 12.9% decline, and in the same period enrolment in educational institutions has fallen by over 54,000 people, a 10.3% decline.[[248]](#cite_note-255)

Latvian policy in science and technology has set out the long-term goal of transitioning from labor-consuming economy to knowledge-based economy.[[249]](#cite_note-256) By 2020 the government aims to spend 1.5% of GDP on research and development, with half of the investments coming from the private sector. Latvia plans to base the development of its scientific potential on existing scientific traditions, particularly in organic chemistry, medical chemistry, genetic engineering, physics, materials science and information technologies.[[250]](#cite_note-257) The greatest number of patents, both nationwide and abroad, are in medical chemistry.[[251]](#cite_note-258) Latvia was ranked 41st in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[252]](#cite_note-259)[[253]](#cite_note-260)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Latvia](/wiki/Health_in_Latvia "Health in Latvia")

The Latvian healthcare system is a [universal programme](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care"), largely funded through government taxation.[[254]](#cite_note-261) It is among the lowest-ranked healthcare systems in Europe, due to excessive waiting times for treatment, insufficient access to the latest medicines, and other factors.[[255]](#cite_note-262) There were 59 hospitals in Latvia in 2009, down from 94 in 2007 and 121 in 2006.[[256]](#cite_note-263)[[257]](#cite_note-264)[[258]](#cite_note-265)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Latvia](/wiki/Culture_of_Latvia "Culture of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Historic_Centre_of_Riga-112610.jpg/250px-Historic_Centre_of_Riga-112610.jpg)](/wiki/File:Historic_Centre_of_Riga-112610.jpg)

The [historic Centre of Riga](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Riga "Historic Centre of Riga") was declared a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 1997.

Traditional Latvian [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"), especially the dance of the [folk songs](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), dates back well over a thousand years. More than 1.2 million texts and 30,000 melodies of folk songs have been identified.[[259]](#cite_note-266)

In the 19th century, Latvian nationalist movements emerged. They promoted Latvian culture and encouraged Latvians to take part in cultural activities. The 19th century and beginning of the 20th century is often regarded by Latvians as a classical era of Latvian culture. Posters show the influence of other European cultures, for example, works of artists such as the Baltic-German artist [Bernhard Borchert](/wiki/Bernhard_Borchert "Bernhard Borchert") and the French [Raoul Dufy](/wiki/Raoul_Dufy "Raoul Dufy").[[260]](#cite_note-267) With the onset of World War II, many Latvian artists and other members of the cultural elite fled the country yet continued to produce their work, largely for a Latvian émigré audience.[[261]](#cite_note-268)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Festival_da_Canci%C3%B3n_Letoa.jpg/250px-Festival_da_Canci%C3%B3n_Letoa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Festival_da_Canci%C3%B3n_Letoa.jpg)

Participants of the Latvian Song and Dance Festival in 2018

The [Latvian Song and Dance Festival](/wiki/Latvian_Song_and_Dance_Festival "Latvian Song and Dance Festival") is an important event in [Latvian](/wiki/Latvians "Latvians") culture and social life. It has been held since 1873, normally every five years. Approximately 30,000 performers altogether participate in the event.[[262]](#cite_note-269) Folk songs and classical choir songs are sung, with emphasis on [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") singing, though modern popular songs have recently been incorporated into the repertoire as well.[[263]](#cite_note-270)

After incorporation into the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), Latvian artists and writers were forced to follow the [socialist realism](/wiki/Socialist_realism "Socialist realism") style of art. During the Soviet era, music became increasingly popular, with the most popular being songs from the 1980s. At this time, songs often made fun of the characteristics of Soviet life and were concerned about preserving Latvian identity. This aroused popular protests against the USSR and also gave rise to an increasing popularity of poetry. Since independence, theatre, [scenography](/wiki/Scenography "Scenography"), [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir") music, and classical music have become the most notable branches of Latvian culture.[[264]](#cite_note-271)

During July 2014, [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") hosted the eighth [World Choir Games](/wiki/World_Choir_Games "World Choir Games") as it played host to over 27,000 choristers representing over 450 choirs and over 70 countries. The festival is the biggest of its kind in the world and is held every two years in a different host city.[[265]](#cite_note-272)

Starting in 2019 Latvia hosts the inaugural [Riga Jurmala Music Festival](https://riga-jurmala.com/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20210702105201/https://riga-jurmala.com/) 2 July 2021 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), a new festival in which world-famous orchestras and conductors perform across four weekends during the summer. The festival takes place at the [Latvian National Opera](/wiki/Latvian_National_Opera "Latvian National Opera"), the Great Guild, and the Great and Small Halls of the Dzintari Concert Hall. This year features the [Bavarian Radio Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Bavarian_Radio_Symphony_Orchestra "Bavarian Radio Symphony Orchestra"), the [Israel Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Israel_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Israel Philharmonic Orchestra"), the [London Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/London_Symphony_Orchestra "London Symphony Orchestra") and the Russian National Orchestra.[[266]](#cite_note-273)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Latvian cuisine](/wiki/Latvian_cuisine "Latvian cuisine")

Latvian cuisine typically consists of agricultural products, with meat featuring in most main meal dishes. Fish is commonly consumed due to Latvia's location on the Baltic Sea. Latvian cuisine has been influenced by neighbouring countries. Common ingredients in Latvian recipes are found locally, such as potatoes, wheat, barley, cabbage, onions, eggs, and pork. Latvian food is generally quite fatty and uses few spices.[[267]](#cite_note-274)

Grey peas with speck are generally considered as staple foods of Latvians. [Sorrel soup](/wiki/Sorrel_soup "Sorrel soup") (*skābeņu zupa*) is also consumed by Latvians.[[268]](#cite_note-275) Rye bread is considered the national [staple](/wiki/Staple_food "Staple food").[[269]](#cite_note-276)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Latvia](/wiki/Sport_in_Latvia "Sport in Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Arena_Riga_CAN-CZE-2006-05-14.JPG/250px-Arena_Riga_CAN-CZE-2006-05-14.JPG)](/wiki/File:Arena_Riga_CAN-CZE-2006-05-14.JPG)

[Arena Riga](/wiki/Arena_Riga "Arena Riga") during the 2006 IIHF World Championship

Ice hockey is usually considered the most popular sport in Latvia. Latvia has had many famous hockey stars like [Helmuts Balderis](/wiki/Helmuts_Balderis "Helmuts Balderis"), [Artūrs Irbe](/wiki/Art%C5%ABrs_Irbe "Artūrs Irbe"), [Kārlis Skrastiņš](/wiki/K%C4%81rlis_Skrasti%C5%86%C5%A1 "Kārlis Skrastiņš") and [Sandis Ozoliņš](/wiki/Sandis_Ozoli%C5%86%C5%A1 "Sandis Ozoliņš") and more recently [Zemgus Girgensons](/wiki/Zemgus_Girgensons "Zemgus Girgensons"), whom the Latvian people have strongly supported in international and NHL play, expressed through the dedication of using the NHL's All Star Voting to bring Zemgus to number one in voting.[[270]](#cite_note-277) [Dinamo Riga](/wiki/Dinamo_Riga "Dinamo Riga") is the country's strongest hockey club, playing in the [Latvian Hockey Higher League](/wiki/Latvian_Hockey_Higher_League "Latvian Hockey Higher League"). The national tournament is the [Latvian Hockey Higher League](/wiki/Latvian_Hockey_Higher_League "Latvian Hockey Higher League"), held since 1931. The [2006 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2006_IIHF_World_Championship "2006 IIHF World Championship") was held in Riga.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Kristaps_Porzingis.jpg/250px-Kristaps_Porzingis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kristaps_Porzingis.jpg)

Kristaps Porziņģis

The second most popular sport is basketball. Latvia has a long basketball tradition, as the [Latvian national basketball team](/wiki/Latvia_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Latvia men's national basketball team") won the first ever [EuroBasket](/wiki/EuroBasket "EuroBasket") in [1935](/wiki/EuroBasket_1935 "EuroBasket 1935") and silver medals in [1939](/wiki/EuroBasket_1939 "EuroBasket 1939"), after losing the final to [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Lithuania men's national basketball team") by one point. Latvia has had many European basketball stars like [Jānis Krūmiņš](/wiki/J%C4%81nis_Kr%C5%ABmi%C5%86%C5%A1 "Jānis Krūmiņš"), [Maigonis Valdmanis](/wiki/Maigonis_Valdmanis "Maigonis Valdmanis"), [Valdis Muižnieks](/wiki/Valdis_Mui%C5%BEnieks "Valdis Muižnieks"), [Valdis Valters](/wiki/Valdis_Valters "Valdis Valters"), [Igors Miglinieks](/wiki/Igors_Miglinieks "Igors Miglinieks"), as well as the first Latvian [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") player [Gundars Vētra](/wiki/Gundars_V%C4%93tra "Gundars Vētra"). [Andris Biedriņš](/wiki/Andris_Biedri%C5%86%C5%A1 "Andris Biedriņš") is one of the most well-known Latvian basketball players, who played in the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") for the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") and the [Utah Jazz](/wiki/Utah_Jazz "Utah Jazz"). Current [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") players include [Kristaps Porziņģis](/wiki/Kristaps_Porzi%C5%86%C4%A3is "Kristaps Porziņģis"), who plays for the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics"), [Dāvis Bertāns](/wiki/D%C4%81vis_Bert%C4%81ns "Dāvis Bertāns"), who plays for the [Oklahoma City Thunder](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Thunder "Oklahoma City Thunder"), and [Rodions Kurucs](/wiki/Rodions_Kurucs "Rodions Kurucs"), who last played for the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks"). Former Latvian basketball club [Rīgas ASK](/wiki/R%C4%ABgas_ASK "Rīgas ASK") won the [Euroleague](/wiki/Euroleague_Basketball "Euroleague Basketball") tournament three times in a row before becoming defunct. Currently, [VEF Rīga](/wiki/BK_VEF_R%C4%ABga "BK VEF Rīga"), which competes in [EuroCup](/wiki/Eurocup_Basketball "Eurocup Basketball"), is the strongest professional basketball club in Latvia. [BK Ventspils](/wiki/BK_Ventspils "BK Ventspils"), which participates in [EuroChallenge](/wiki/EuroChallenge "EuroChallenge"), is the second strongest basketball club in Latvia, previously winning [LBL](/wiki/Latvijas_Basketbola_l%C4%ABga "Latvijas Basketbola līga") eight times and [BBL](/wiki/Baltic_Basketball_League "Baltic Basketball League") in 2013.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Latvia was one of the [EuroBasket 2015](/wiki/EuroBasket_2015 "EuroBasket 2015") hosts and will be one of the hosts once again in [2025](/wiki/EuroBasket_2025 "EuroBasket 2025").

Other popular sports include [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [floorball](/wiki/Floorball "Floorball"), tennis, volleyball, cycling, [bobsleigh](/wiki/Bobsleigh "Bobsleigh") and [skeleton](/wiki/Skeleton_(sport) "Skeleton (sport)"). The [Latvian national football team](/wiki/Latvia_national_football_team "Latvia national football team")'s only major [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") tournament participation has been the [2004 UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004").[[271]](#cite_note-278)

[Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_at_the_Olympics "Latvia at the Olympics") has participated successfully in both [Winter](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") and [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics"). The most successful Olympic athlete in the history of independent Latvia has been [Māris Štrombergs](/wiki/M%C4%81ris_%C5%A0trombergs "Māris Štrombergs"), who became a two-time Olympic champion in 2008 and 2012 at Men's BMX.[[272]](#cite_note-279)

In Boxing, [Mairis Briedis](/wiki/Mairis_Briedis "Mairis Briedis") is the first and only Latvian to date, to win a boxing world title, having held the WBC cruiserweight title from 2017 to 2018, the WBO cruiserweight title in 2019, and the IBF / The Ring magazine cruiserweight titles in 2020.

In 2017, Latvian tennis player [Jeļena Ostapenko](/wiki/Je%C4%BCena_Ostapenko "Jeļena Ostapenko") won the [2017 French Open Women's singles title](/wiki/2017_French_Open_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_Singles "2017 French Open – Women's Singles"), being the first unseeded player to do so in the open era.

In futsal, Latvia will host the [UEFA Futsal Euro 2026](/wiki/UEFA_Futsal_Euro_2026 "UEFA Futsal Euro 2026") alongside Lithuania, their national team will make their debut as co-host.
